CS2 is not a game.

Would recommend: Unfortunately.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 isn’t just a video game; it’s an interactive anger management test you are mathematically guaranteed to fail. Valve somehow took the digital equivalent of stubbing your toe on a coffee table, added gorgeous dynamic volumetric smoke, and sold it back to us as entertainment.

You’ll really enjoy this game if you meet the following criteria:

You find yourself questioning your life choices on a regular basis
You have little patience for the people around you
You thrive in highly competitive and sometimes toxic environments
Your reflexes are sharp, but strategic thinking isn’t exactly your strong suit

Welcome to the premier self-flagellation simulator of our generation. If your idea of a relaxing evening is getting your digital head blown off from across the map by a teenager who then critiques your gameplay over a $10 microphone, this is your forever home.

CS2 strips away the illusion that you are a good team player. "Strategy" mostly consists of four strangers screaming contradictory instructions at you while you try to clutch a 1v3. It is a beautiful, lawless wasteland where you learn fascinating, imaginative insults in languages you don’t speak. You don't play this game to have fun; you play because you harbor deep, unyielding resentment for your blood pressure and want to share that misery with nine other unfortunate souls.

It's a chaotic void that actively rewards whoever has the quickest trigger finger and the lowest sense of personal decency.

10/10. It's an absolute nightmare. See you in the matchmaking queue tomorrow.

text:
The biggest issue by far is cheating, but it's far from the only problem.

Compared to CS, the gunplay in CS2 often feels like a downgrade. Spray patterns that veteran players spent years mastering now feel far less consistent and predictable, making fights feel less skill-based than they should. Combined with questionable hit registration, there are too many moments where shots appear to connect but don't register as expected. In a game that has built its reputation on precision, consistency, and rewarding mechanical skill, these issues are impossible to ignore.

For years, the Counter-Strike community has complained about hackers, and CS2 has done little to restore faith in the competitive experience. Whether it's blatant rage hackers, wallhackers trying to hide their cheats, or players using subtle assistance that is difficult to prove, cheating continues to be one of the most discussed problems in the game. The fact that so many players feel forced to use FACEIT rather than Valve's own matchmaking says everything about the state of competitive integrity.

No game should require a third-party client, additional anti-cheat software, and in some cases identity verification just to have a reasonable expectation of a fair match. The existence of FACEIT should be an optional competitive platform, not the community's unofficial solution to Valve's inability to maintain trust in its own matchmaking ecosystem.

Valve's Trust Factor system is another source of frustration. Rather than directly removing cheaters from the game, the system often feels like it simply attempts to separate players into different pools. For legitimate players, this creates the impression that they must spend countless hours grinding matches, maintaining account standing, or investing further into the Steam ecosystem just to escape the worst matchmaking experiences. Even after reaching what many would consider a good Trust Factor, suspicious players, covert wallhackers, and obvious cheaters remain common complaints.

What makes the situation even more frustrating is that Valve is one of the most profitable companies in gaming. Players constantly see updates, cases, skins, and marketplace activity, yet fundamental issues affecting competitive integrity continue to linger. It creates the perception that cosmetic monetization receives more attention than the actual health of the game. At times, it feels like Valve is far more interested in keeping the skin economy thriving than fixing the problems that directly affect gameplay.

The Counter-Strike community often gives Valve a level of leniency that would never be extended to companies like EA, Ubisoft, or Activision. If any of those publishers released a competitive shooter with similar cheating concerns, anti-cheat controversies, unreliable hit registration, and matchmaking frustrations, they would be relentlessly criticized. Valve deserves the same level of accountability.

The saddest part is that Counter-Strike 2 remains popular largely because there is no true alternative. There is still no major 5v5 tactical shooter that captures the simplicity and purity of classic Counter-Strike without adding heroes, abilities, or other mechanics. Many players continue playing not because they love the current state of CS2, but because there is nowhere else to go.

Counter-Strike 2 is a game built on one of the greatest competitive formulas ever created. Unfortunately, that formula is being undermined by persistent cheating concerns, a questionable Trust Factor system, unreliable hit registration, inconsistent spray control, and a growing sense that the priorities of the developer no longer align with the priorities of the players.

Rating: 3/10

text:
I've been playing Counter-Strike since CS 1.3, and I've never seen the cheating situation this bad.

Deathmatch is full of blatant spinbotters and aimbotters, even on official servers. Premier, the game's flagship competitive mode, often feels like a coin flip on who gets the cheater. After watching demos, it's hard not to conclude that cheating has become a normal part of the experience.

CS2 still has the potential to be a great game, but until Valve seriously addresses the anti-cheat problem, I can't recommend it.

text: At first I thought the game was amazing for building and flexing skill, but after over 300 hours on the game I have come to realize that most of your performance on CS2 comes down to how good your internet connection is, and how close to the server you are. When you have a decent connection, everything hits. But when you verse kids with 1 to 12 ping while you're at 25+ with some net-jitter sprinkled on top, GOOD LUCK. Enemy's will eat 5-7 bullets but kill you in 1. If you're playing on wifi you're always at a disadvantage on a game like this. Behind the tick issues, the second biggest issue on CS2, are the cheaters. Mostly the togglers, they are good at hiding the fact they cheat but the statistics are according to CS2 cheat developers is that there are 1 in 10 players cheating in-game. That's ~2 per casual lobby. Yes, that's right. Not everyone we verse ACTUALLY has the skill of DONK, no. They're big f4t, cheaters who love to pretend like they don't cheat, yet they all have this weird egotistical attitude which outs them immediately. Don't be fooled by the cheat duo's either. There's a bunch of little cheater groups that match-make together and try to hide the fact they're cheating. One time one of these little rats cheat software screwed up and exposed that they were in-fact 100% cheating, valve did end up banning them by the end of the game... but that's the only time i've seen a bunch of reports lead to an immediate ban. I think this is a very difficult game because it is mainly suited for people who are able to understand and recognize the game's mechanics. Most players online do not necessarily focus on the mechanics and aiming, even though these are extremely important aspects of Counter-Strike.

As for the company itself, I believe it does little to nothing to prevent cheating, and it may even have an interest in not addressing the issue properly. Over the years, it has become clear that their anti-cheat system is extremely poor and is not updated frequently enough, which allows cheating and exploits to remain widespread in the game.

I have another account where I also purchased Counter-Strike, and on that account I could definitely feel how terrible this experience can be. I experienced the same problem on this account when I first started playing, and it only improved after I accumulated a large number of hours in the game.

Furthermore, Steam's response was that it is not possible to manually influence your Trust Factor because it is generated automatically by a system that enforces the rules and calculates a Trust Factor score. used to encounter a cheater every few games, but now it’s rare to find one that doesn’t have cheaters. The game has officially become unplayable for me, which is really sad because it's been my favorite game since its release.

At this point, I’m deleting it until there are significant updates to the anti-cheat system. The situation has gotten out of control. Cheaters have become so bold and obvious with their methods, and it seems like they face no consequences. The report button might as well not exist—I constantly see the same cheater names over and over again, which suggests no bans are being enforced.

What’s even more frustrating (and honestly kind of funny in a messed-up way) is that I sometimes get banned from matchmaking for hours because of the VAC system, despite having done absolutely nothing. Meanwhile, actual cheaters are running rampant with no consequences! Honestly, it feels like the VAC system is either completely broken, or it desn't really exist to begin with!!

It’s baffling to me (and I might be naive here) that something as blatant as 100% headshots or players knowing everyone’s location on the map isn’t being detected by the system. There should be stricter measures in place, like a kill limit (say 4 or 5 kills) that triggers an automatic review or stricter bans system, especially when multiple players are reporting the same person for cheating.

Sadly, I don’t think Valve is putting any effort into fixing this issue. Why would they, when the game is still making so much money? But the reality is that for regular players such as myself, the game has become overrun by cheaters, making it completely unplayable for me.

Honestly, having been away from Counter-Strike since 1.5-1.6 in my high school years 20 years ago and being extremely competitive, playing in CAL-I and CPL... I was ecstatic when I realized Counter-Strike 2 existed after recently getting a gaming pc after decades without and thus, no pc gaming.  

The game feels incredible, pure nostalgia, without any of the weird tink tink tin feeling and gameplay of CS:Source/CS:GO. This feels like a return to almost how things felt back before Steam or CS:Source even existed, back on the Valve/Half Life engine. I'm still working to maintain consistent 1:1 KD which is humbling, and trying to figure out my old settings and sensitivity calibration has been a chore, but it's worth it now that I've got it. 

BIG RED FLAG: I don't cheat or hack, I don't even know how. With the weird VAC setup they have, if you are in a team with somebody who gets flagged by VAC for cheating, and it was even a matchmaking team, nobody knew the person, we all got 20 hour bans just by being ON THEIR SAME TEAM!!! That is atrocious. There are so many people so blatantly hacking on the highest competitive mode: Premier, and that's also where this happened and just the player's teammates through matchmaking even got 20hr "cooldowns" like we had endorsed the random teammate's hacking or something. It's awful. Truly guilty by association. Except the association is that the game I chose to play on my one day off in two weeks was the one to match me with this person in the first place for competitive matchmaking... only to punish all 4 of his teammates with absurd 20 hour bans for being TEAMMATES!! 

Many things like ^ having to do with a terrible rate of hacking in the highest competitive tier will absolutely enrage you.

text:
Got into this game a bit late, but I like it nonetheless. The number of hackers can be really frustrating though, and it often feels like Valve doesn't do anything about them. Coming from Valorant, where cheating is almost impossible and the main thing you had to worry about was whether your teammates are good, it was a pretty big adjustment for me. In CS, especially when I first started, it often felt like I had to figure out whether someone was cheating before I could just focus on enjoying the game.
That said, once you get past that, the gameplay experience is kinda good. The gunplay, movement, and overall skill ceiling feel far more rewarding, and every match feels differen and not to mention the MAPS are really scenic. The skin system also adds a fun collecting and trading aspect that keeps things interesting outside of just playing. Despite its issues, there's a reason the game has stayed relevant for so many years, when CS is at its best, there's nothing quite like it. And also the E-Sports is way way way better and entertaining to watch compared to other similar games..

text:
Do you know what disgusts me most? It isn't defeat. It isn't competition. It isn't even failure. It is corruption without purpose. Parasites who hide behind software, scripts, and stolen advantages. Cheaters in Counter-Strike 2 are not competitors. They are cowards. They possess no discipline, no skill, no honor. They poison every match they enter and call it victory.

A city cannot survive when criminals rule its streets. And a game cannot survive when cheaters rule its servers. Every wallhack, every aimbot, every exploit is another crack in the foundation. Honest players invest thousands of hours mastering their craft, only to have their efforts stolen by those too weak to earn victory themselves.

These people do not deserve sympathy. They deserve removal. Permanent. Absolute. Let them watch from the outside while real players compete. Counter-Strike was built on skill, trust, and competition. Cheaters threaten all three. And when something threatens the future of an empire, it must be eliminated. Not because of anger. Not because of revenge. Because order demands it.

text:
CS2 is easily one of the best games I have ever played. I've played many games over the years, but CS2 is the one that keeps bringing me back. The gameplay is competitive, exciting, and every match feels different.

The game isn't perfect. Sometimes shots that look like they should hit don't register, and there are occasional bugs or strange moments that can be frustrating. I'm not always sure whether those issues come from my PC, internet connection, or the game itself. However, these problems are usually small compared to the overall experience.

What makes CS2 special is its skill-based gameplay. Winning a round feels rewarding, improving your aim feels satisfying, and every clutch moment creates unforgettable memories. The weapon mechanics, maps, and competitive atmosphere make it one of the most addictive and enjoyable FPS games available today.

Despite its flaws, I genuinely love CS2. It's more than just a game for me—it's a game I've spent countless hours enjoying, learning, and improving at. For me, CS2 is one of the greatest competitive shooters ever made.

Rating: 9/10 ⭐

text:
So I nearly played a thousand hours in this game and I'm ready to make my review, say my impressions and generally tell how i feel after playing it, hope it will help people who are reading this (There's a lot of things to say so prepare a drink or something in the meantime).

I never played CS:GO, source or 1.6 but after playing this game I kow the kind of enjoyment it procures for me, mecanically it's a great game, if you make a mistake it's on you, but learning how to counter strafe, peak, how to correctly place my cross-hair is something you want to strive for as you want to improve. Counterstrike is a mecanically difficult game but once you see how good or great players play you will understand step by step and how to generally understand what's happening in the many rounds you will have and how to make the best decisions for you and your teammates. The Pro scene is also incredible to watch and entertaining, to see how incredible players play and how they understand the game and apply their strategies, the decisions they make, some have a flashy style some have a more passive style but its fun and great to see and watch masters in their domain. I also like how simple yet difficult the game is, there is no drones, no special powers but you need good mecanical skills and coordination with your team (that many times fails) in order to improve and win rounds. I know that a lot of people will think wow this guys lives in a fantasy world outside the boundaries of reality dont worry we will get to the more darkest and worst parts of this game but since CS2 is my first person tactical shooter I still enjoyed the moments and strived to improve my skills, learn new utilities line ups in order to have more chances of victory ecc... 

As to why CS2 is for me a good yet controversial game is for 4 main reasons:

1- The game sometimes feels off and what I'm trying to say is that if you counterstrafe correctly with no issues or just shoot at the ennemy while your staying still placing your cross hair right into the head sometimes you will not get a confirmed kill, personally I don't have much of those issues but only sometimes, I'm sure its because I have a PC with good specs, but a lot of good players with good specs on their pc or not will tell the same thing about this. So I recommend to have good internet and a fairly good pc if you want to truly enjoy the game and not allways get frustrated. ( In this case it is not your fault if you did not won the duel against your opponent) I also personnally miss a lot of shots and throw a lot of opportunities.

2- The second issue is also about the game, sometimes the game will randomly lag and you'll experience a huge drop of FPS, sometimes the game wont start and you need to restart it or sometimes the game will slow you down with some bugs (cant charge 1v1 duels or join someone ecc.)

3- The third main reason is not about the game anymore but about the community that is also a bit controversial there is a part of the community that works a lot for the game to live on, with workshop maps where you can train yourself, warm up or even play a lot of different mods alone or with friends in special servers. But if you want to play premier (ranked) or competitive a lot of individuals are toxic or will just insult you just can't communicate with because they dont speak english. People who will lunch premier to only grief, team kill or hinder you. So I recommend that if you are the type of person to tilt or get angry easily then this game is not for you and its better to not play it for your mental health. Even though I met a lot of individuals like that I also met great people and laughed together and continued to play together so I recommend that if you are new to FPS play with an acquaintance who already plays this game it will help you a lot. 

4 - The fourth main reason is the anticheat that is clearly NOT WORKING, you can have in your games spinbots hackers, wallhackers who have every piece of info, aimbot hackers who will allways lock their target in your head no matter the situation, cheaters with bunnyhop scripts, cheaters with radar hack where they can know every move you do in the map and streamsnipers who also affects a lot the streamers community. This part of the game is really frustrating (but also brings happiness and joy when you beat them in many rounds a cheater or a team of cheaters because yes those who use cheats in CS2 are WASHED at the game) The game is basically infested with cheaters in any mode you want to play competitive, death match or premier. This problem is massive considering the importance of counterstrike and its playerbase and I know Valve has the funds and all the necessary things to make a good anticheat but they still decide to do nothing so I think its voluntary because there is no other way to answer that question as to why there is not a good anticheat. BTW you can check profiles of players to see if they are clean by typing "rip" or "tips" in the place of .com or "csteam" or "xsteam" at the begining of the link of the players account. This fourth reason is one of the biggest reasons why it leaves a bitter taste in my mouth and I'm starting to not enjoy as I enjoyed it in the beginniing so an advice from me is that the best thing to do is not take this game seriously even if you are playing ranked it is better to laugh it off instead of getting angry over people demeanor or cheaters. 

So dear readers I hope it will help you in your journey and made you think twice before learning russian and turkish insults (joke). Personally I still think it is a good game but has way too many issues to truly enjoy it and strive to grind the game, after discovering counterstrike and its history in the esport league I got compltely hooked and enjoyed watching majors and many differents pro leagues events and also I still enjoy the game a little bit. I do recommend that if you are the type of person who likes those aspects then welcome but to other people it is better for your bloodpressure and your sanity to not even start playing it. CounterStrike is like a beautiful goddess of insanity that whispers kind words into your ears trying to lure you in a sweet honey trap. The other controversial part is that in order to not play with or against cheaters you need to install another app called Face.it in order to play with no cheaters and have clean games. 

PS: I will not talk about skins and skins prices since it is a separate matter and of course careful guys while you are purchasing skins or cases, dont use money  you cannot afford to spend on gambling NEVER, also racism is not allowed or others different kinds of insults, be kind to other people even if they dont have a good behavior towards you or your team mates. More tan a tactical shooter it is a game of self control. 

Final result of this review do not recommend if you are a sane person.

text:
Biltong is a traditional South African dried meat snack made by curing strips of meat with salt, vinegar, spices, and then air-drying them slowly. It is similar to jerky, but biltong is usually thicker, less sweet, and cured with vinegar, coriander, black pepper, and salt rather than being cooked or smoked. The result is a rich, savoury, slightly tangy dried meat with a deep meaty flavour.

Biltong can be made from beef, game meat, or venison. In South Africa, game biltong is especially popular because animals such as kudu, springbok, impala, and rooibok produce lean, flavourful meat that dries well. Rooibok biltong has a slightly wild, clean taste and works best when the meat is handled carefully and dried slowly.

Rooibok Biltong Recipe
Ingredients
1 kg rooibok meat, cut into long strips
2 tablespoons coarse salt
1 tablespoon brown sugar
2 tablespoons whole coriander seeds
1 teaspoon black pepper
½ teaspoon bicarbonate of soda, optional
½ cup brown vinegar or apple cider vinegar
1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce, optional
Method

First, trim away any excess fat, sinew, or silver skin from the rooibok meat. Cut the meat with the grain into long strips, about 2 to 4 cm thick, depending on how wet or dry you want the final biltong to be.

Toast the coriander seeds in a dry pan until fragrant, then crush them roughly. Mix the crushed coriander with the salt, brown sugar, black pepper, and bicarbonate of soda if using.

Place the rooibok strips in a shallow dish. Sprinkle the spice mixture over the meat, making sure each piece is evenly coated. Add the vinegar and Worcestershire sauce, then turn the meat so all sides are covered. Cover the dish and refrigerate for 12 to 24 hours.

After marinating, remove the meat and gently pat it dry with paper towel. Do not rinse it, as this will remove too much flavour. Insert hooks through the thicker end of each strip and hang the meat in a biltong box or a cool, dry, well-ventilated space.

Dry the biltong for 3 to 7 days, depending on the thickness of the meat and your preferred texture. For wetter biltong, check after 3 days. For dry biltong, leave it longer until firm throughout.

Once ready, slice the biltong thinly across the grain and store it in a breathable paper bag in the fridge, or vacuum seal it for longer storage.

biltong can be made from beef game meat or venison. in south africa game biltong is especially popular because animals such as kudu springbok impala and rooibok produce lean flavourful meat that dries well. rooibok biltong has a slightly wild clean taste and works best when the meat is handled carefully and dried slowly.

text:
Review

Counter-Strike has been the gold standard of competitive shooters for over two decades, and with Counter-Strike 2, Valve set out to modernize the experience without losing what made the franchise so addictive in the first place. The result is a game that feels familiar in its bones but noticeably sharper, smoother, and more polished on the surface.

Gameplay

The core loop hasn't changed: two teams of five, one side planting a bomb, the other defending the site, rounds won through tactics, aim, and economy management. If you've played CS:GO, you'll feel at home within minutes. What's different is how everything responds. Movement feels tighter, peeking corners is more readable, and smoke grenades are now fully volumetric — they react to gunfire, bullets punch temporary holes through them, and they interact with the environment in ways that add a real layer of strategy to executes and retakes.

Gunplay still rewards precision and discipline over spray-and-pray instincts. Recoil patterns are familiar enough for veterans to adapt quickly, while new players have an easier time reading what's happening on screen thanks to clearer visual feedback.

Graphics and Performance

Built on Source 2, the game looks like a genuine generational leap from its predecessor. Lighting is dynamic, maps have been rebuilt with finer detail, and the overall presentation feels current without being unnecessarily flashy. Despite the visual upgrade, performance is solid — frame rates stay high even on modest hardware, which matters enormously in a game where milliseconds decide duels.

Maps

Classic maps like Mirage, Inferno, Dust II, and Nuke have been rebuilt from the ground up rather than simply re-skinned. They retain their iconic layouts and angles that pros and casuals alike have memorized for years, but with improved lighting and texture work that makes them easier on the eyes during long sessions.

Matchmaking and Anti-Cheat

Matchmaking feels reasonably consistent, though queue times and rank distribution can vary depending on region and time of day. Cheating remains an ongoing battle, as it does in every competitive shooter, but Valve has continued rolling out updates to its detection systems.

Verdict

Counter-Strike 2 doesn't reinvent the wheel — it doesn't need to. It refines a formula that has already proven timeless, wrapping it in better technology and smoother mechanics. For longtime fans, it's an easy transition. For newcomers, it's one of the most rewarding (and unforgiving) competitive shooters you can pick up today.

Rating: 8.5/10 — A worthy evolution of a legendary franchise, held back only by the usual growing pains of cheaters and occasional server hiccups.

text:
CS2 would be a good game if it weren't for the:
--hackers that seem to never get banned
--smurfs who only play in lower ranks
--ragers and griefers that never get banned
--edgelords who think yelling the N-word is cool
--a rating system that doesn't make sense and rises and falls based on your [i]team's[/i] performance rather than your own
--a report system that most certainly doesn't work
--movement that's clunky and unresponsive compared to 1.6
--shots that don't register for no particular reason
--footsteps that can only be heard when right next to you
--net jitter, whatever that means
--gambling game disguised as cosmetics crates, preying on young people
--general indifference to all of the above by the devs

If CS2 wasn't such a unique and popular tactical FPS with a strong esport following and amazing legacy from 1.6 and GO, it would be a fringe game not deserving of any attention.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) — это масштабное и визуально красивое обновление легендарного тактического шутера на движке Source 2, которое принесло как крутые инновации, так и новые проблемы.

Главным триумфом игры стал адаптивный дым, который теперь реалистично заполняет пространство, простреливается и разгоняется гранатами, полностью меняя тактику раундов. Графика, свет и читаемость моделей вышли на современный уровень, а матчи стали динамичнее благодаря переходу на укороченный формат до 13 раундов (MR12).

Однако переход на новый движок дался игре тяжело. Система sub-tick, призванная идеально регистрировать попадания, до сих пор вызывает споры в комьюнити из-за редких задержек, а сама игра стала гораздо требовательнее к железу, лишив комфортного FPS владельцев слабых ПК. Но самой большой проблемой остается слабый античит, из-за которого в соревновательных режимах все еще много читеров, что вынуждает хардкорных геймеров уходить на сторонние платформы вроде Faceit.

В итоге CS2 — это отличный, современный фундамент для будущего франшизы, который все еще находится в процессе полировки. Альтернатив такого уровня на рынке нет, но Valve предстоит проделать еще много работы над оптимизацией и безопасностью. Оценка: 7.5/10.

text:
This game is absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I swear to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ god, every single morning I wake up, smoke a cigarette, drink my coffee, and like a complete, terminal idiot, I click on this radioactive piece of unoptimized garbage. How do you take csgo, a game that is almost 15 years old, ACTUALLY WORKED, and had real soul, throw it directly into the trash just to force feed us this unplayable, plastic dumpster fire of a "game".
What is this disgusting ass lighting? Why does every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ map look like a sterile, over saturated mobile game filled with fake, blinding colors that make my eyes bleed. It hurts my eyes, my retinas are burning. And for what? So their brainless developers can change the map angles every two weeks for absolutely no reason? They tweak geometry nobody asked them to touch while the core game is literally melting.
And the people... holy mother of ♥♥♥♥, the absolute brainrot in the lobbies. Every single match is a psychological torment in how much pure human stupidity one man can endure before his ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ head explodes. I am queued with actual mentally retarded subhumans. I am convinced these people belong in a locked asylum, playing with their monitors turned off, drooling on their keyboards, with their mice plugged directly into a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ watermelon. The matchmaking system is a pathetic, sick joke, either I play against five no life, sweating tryhards who haven’t seen daylight since 2018, or I get saddled with complete monkeys who don't even know which side of the grenade to throw.
And the voice chat. A LITERAL GEOPOLITICAL WARZONE. A non stop, ear bleeding, screeching symphony of russians, turkish, kazakhs, belarusians, and ukrainians screaming into 2 euro microphones they clearly dug out of a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ dumpster. The exact millisecond you open your mouth to say "stop rushing a and dying like idiots," these mentally ill ♥♥♥♥♥ start foaming at the mouth. They will scream their lungs out, calling you a cheater or a troll, while they themselves are actively body blocking or locking onto an enemy's head through a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ brick wall.
WHICH BRINGS ME TO VAC. THIS ANTICHEAT IS A USELESS, DECORATIVE PIECE OF CODE. IT DOES NOT DO A SINGLE ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ THING. You have wallhacks, and absolute scum tracking your head through three layers of concrete, laughing in your face. Meanwhile, this brainless ai system bans legitimate people just because they updated their gpu drivers or flicked their mouse too fast (wasn't my case but I've seen people have this problem). It bans innocent people for existing, while actual closet cheating monkeys are running around completely unbothered, and the second you manage to snipe them with an ssg twice, they instantly call you for cheating and mass report you just so they can keep cheating in this pathetic ass excuse of a game.
Why did I even buy Prime? For the weekly drops? Wow, thank you valve, you greedy, corporate parasites. A 3 cent graffiti and a battle scarred skin that looks like someone dragged it through mud and took a ♥♥♥♥ on it. Truly worth my money.
The only thing keeping my fried ass dopamine receptors alive is the gambling, but the game's own case system is a total scam. The odds are garbage. You are 10 times better off going to sketchy, degenerate ass third party gambling sites to open cases or roll roulette. At least those sites give valve absolutely zero money, which is exactly what these corporate thieves deserve.
Everything is a massive, flaming downgrade. The subtick feels like playing underwater, the movement is heavy like I’m wearing concrete boots, the audio is a guessing game, and this community makes me want to burn my PC and move to a cabin in the woods.
I HATE THIS GAME WITH EVERY SINGLE FIBER OF MY MISERABLE SOUL. IT IS A BROKEN, CHEATER-INFESTED PIECE OF TRASH.
Anyway, the queue finished, I just loaded into a match, and I am already opening a case on a third party site on my second monitor. 10/10, I am ready to lose another 10 matches in a row, see you in-game.

text:
The last evolution of the good old Counter-Strike game, the absolute jewel of Valve's crown, is definitely an experience. While it brings that gorgeous modern facelift, the classic, cutthroat tactical shooter loop we've known for decades remains fully intact.

Pros:
⭐ The graphics now finally feel like they belong in the current era, giving the game a beautiful, modern look.
⭐ The maps are very well executed; they still feel completely familiar like the old days, but some layouts actually look and play much better now.
⭐ The dynamic volumetric smokes are absolutely amazing. The way they interact with bullets and explosions completely changes the tactical depth.

Cons:
❌ Cheaters are honestly everywhere right now, and it can get incredibly frustrating to deal with them so often.
❌ The matchmaking sometimes feels completely unbalanced, putting you into matches that feel entirely one-sided.
❌ Toxic players are everywhere, making communication a massive gamble if you aren't playing with a full lobby of friends.

In the end, CS2 still firmly holds its place as the definitive king of competitive multiplayer shooter games.

Final Score for now: 7.5 / 10 ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

text: Counter Strike is the greatest First Person Shooter multiplayer franchise to ever exist. It's inception is indebted to the community, and likewise the community is tethered to Valve's staunchly laissez faire approach to maintaining and propelling the game. It's as simple as you want it or as deep as you demand it to be. I think cs2 needs more optimization care to truly be a great installment but simultaneously the jump to source 2 was needed, and I cannot imagine going back to the old state of smokes/he's. There is also a bit of a content drought, but if you are a fan of tac fps's or search and destroy and you are not playing this game, I don't know what you are doing. text:
Is cs an amazing game as esports? yeah. is it worth picking up? not really.

new players will have issues with aggressive farming bots in gungame, deathmatch and casual modes which are so bad that real players can't keep up. 
players with newish steam accounts or small amount of games or money spent will have lower trust factor. 
if you are starting strong skillwise you will get reported by enemy team in placing matches, so trust factor will be even lower then, which means you will be put against suspicious players like smurfs that are boosting accounts or their friends' accounts or even blatant cheaters. 

you are new? -get slammed by case farming bots
you are new and good at fps games? -placed in low trust factor with smurfing nerds and cheaters
you've calibrated low in premier and trying to level up? don't try too hard, or you will get reported and put on naughty list

game is hard to master and that's why it's fun. ingame volvo severs, anticheat and rating systems are pretty painful. there's some fun to get in default modes, but if you want to be competitive just really go faceit premium.

text:
The game is awesome, I think the best shooter among FPS games right now.
BUT, there is a huge drawback: the developers do not care about the principles of fair play they themselves write about. They tell us:
In all modes:
- Do not use cheats;
- Do not interfere with the game or insult teammates or opponents;
- Do not use software that automatically performs any actions.
In Competitive and Teammates modes:
- Play to win;*
- Play till the end.
There's a huge number cheaters, making it impossible to have good matches. They have a huge variety third-party software (aim, wallhack, bhop, etc.). But, VAC either doesn`t work properly or doesn`t work at all. A big minus to the developers for not addressing this issue, even though they have opportunities to do so. Another disadvantage is that you have to download a third-party client called FACEIT to play in a truly fair environment. Newcomers are faced with a terrible experience; they won't enjoy the game and will likely quickly quit. Moreover, a new player will probably get the bad Trust Factor, that means a player will play with more cheaters and people with unappropriate behavior.
This review won't be positive until justice is prevailed and cheaters are punished alongside with their friends and people who order their services. And I haven`t even listed the other problems and drawbacks that also negatively affect the gameplay and make the impression that developers don`t care about their players.

text:
CS isn't just a game on my Steam library; it's a time machine. Every time I boot it up, I’m instantly transported back to those chaotic late-night LAN parties and noisy internet cafes where the air was thick with adrenaline.
The sheer thrill of a 1v5 clutch still gets my heart racing like no other game can. The sound of the AWP shot, the frantic callouts, and the sheer joy of a perfectly executed rush—it’s pure magic. But what truly keeps me coming back is the "dorhami" (togetherness). Gathering with my old squad, laughing at our fails, and celebrating those insane highlight reels—those moments are priceless and irreplaceable.

However, I’d be lying if I said this game was perfect. The elephant in the room is, and has always been, the cheaters. We've been dealing with spin-botters and wall-hackers for years—since the 1.6 days, honestly. It’s incredibly frustrating when a tight, competitive match gets ruined by someone who just doesn't want to play fair, and it’s a shame that after all this time, it’s still a persistent stain on the community.

Locking through walls, turning 180 degrees and preaiming before you can see the opposing player. Full tracking, dodging grenades without seeing them (matrix style) etc.
It's complete crap show from top to bottom. Deathmatch is even worse. In some lobbies 20 out of 26/28 people are bots. Some bots are with 360 fov headshot spam. So have fun. The market is also overrun by bot farmers to inflate prices. If you look at weapon count for most weapons the amount in existence is higher than active player count (which stands at 500K) by a factor of two at least. So how do those skins gain value? Bot farmers buy them all up, because you won't see most skins in the game any time. So where do they exist? In some bot farmers stashes.

And the worst part is that Valve encourages it actively. You can't hit financial targets by banning bots/cheaters. Can you? So this is how this goes. Bots farm and sell cases for gamblers. Then buy all play skins and pump stuff up in skin markets with the proceeds.  You can't see skins at 3000 elo, 5000 elo, 7000 elo, 10k elo, 13k elo, 15k elo. Sometimes you can meet a person with an inventory (not a well build inventory, but just an invetory), but they are far in between. So the A4M4 that should cost 2 USD, costs 30 USD and players end up paying bot farmers for their work. It's not wonder almost all manipulation comes from China, who runs bot farms.

I am not even going start about 128 tick vs sub tick garbage and all the problems that arise from that. Anyone who has worked with math and extrapolation knows, that extrapolation accuracy is non-existent, and never will be. If you don't believe me try investing on extrapolation. You will end up bankrupt ASAP. Shot bullets disappearing, not registering even in LAN. I saw ROPZ playing Lan and aiming straight in to the head of opposing player shooting and missing and that's lan... While completely missed shots land. While training with bots I encountered many times, where multiple bots run in a row, second and third bots die, but first one survives hit in the face from desert eagle. Worse is that I believe it's actually hardcoded into the game, the missing bullets I mean. If you shoot m4a4, m4a1, galil etc. you will encounter that shooting straight into the head, the second or first bullet disappears. Sometimes direct headshot from m4a4 the second bullet won't register headshot or disappear totally. So to score a headshot kill from m4a4 you need to shoot 3 bullets... There is a reason all Pros grab AK-47, the moment they can get their hands on it. If you shoot two bullets with AK47 into opponents head, one will register and it will be a kill. Not 3 bullets needed. How pathetic is that?

Verdict:
Don't recommend. Don't spend money on game until fixed.

text:
With the launch of Premier Mode, Valve aimed to bring the professional tournament experience directly to the casual and hardcore player alike, reducing the community's reliance on third-party platforms like FACEIT.

The Pick/Ban Phase
Premier Mode introduces a sleek veto system where teams take turns banning maps until a single battleground is left. This forces players to expand their map pool rather than just grinding Mirage or Dust II all day.

The CS Rating & Leaderboards
Instead of the classic hidden Elo ranks (Silver, Gold Nova, Global Elite), Premier utilizes a transparent numerical CS Rating (e.g., 12,500, 21,000) paired with regional and global leaderboards.

The Good: You know exactly how many points you stand to win or lose before the match even starts.

The Bad: The classic competitive mode still exists but tracks ranks individually per map, which fractured the player base and made traditional ranks feel less prestigious.

text:
The current state of the anti-cheat system in Counter-Strike 2 is a critical failure that completely compromises the competitive ecosystem and completely negates all development efforts. The absolute incompetence and impotence of the protective algorithms have turned competitive matchmaking into a total farce. Instead of fair play, players face rampant third-party software usage that the VAC system is simply incapable of detecting.
Continuous failure to detect even the most obvious, blatant cheats demonstrates the fundamental structural inadequacy of the entire security architecture. The modified protection systems on Source 2, marketed as a cutting-edge technological barrier, have proven to be purely cosmetic and utterly useless. Infrequent and isolated ban waves do not solve the problem; they merely confirm negligence during network code design and the developers' helplessness. Valve's inaction destabilizes the platform and thoroughly destroys community trust. The current anti-cheat system completely strips CS2 of its status as a legitimate esport discipline, demanding an immediate, radical overhaul before the platform's degradation becomes irreversible.

text: I grew up playing this franchise. It shaped my childhood, fueled my passion for gaming, and ultimately became my career. Transitioning from a kid playing for fun to competing as a professional player gives me a unique perspective on Counter-Strike 2.Valve had a massive task in replacing CS:GO, and while the transition hasn't been flawless, CS2 successfully modernizes the greatest tactical shooter of all time.The ProsStrategic Evolution: Volumetric smoke physics completely rewrite the tactical playbook. Displacing smoke with HE grenades adds incredible depth to professional play.Visual Clarity: The Source 2 engine provides vibrant lighting and vastly improved player visibility, which is crucial for high-level competitive integrity.Audio Engineering: Sound cues are sharper and more directional, allowing for precise, instinctual audio positioning.The ConsSubtick Consistency: From a professional standpoint, the subtick system still lacks the crisp, predictable responsiveness of legacy 128-tick servers.Performance Optimization: Higher hardware demands can cause occasional frame-rate drops, requiring a high-end PC to maintain stable tournament-level performance.Final VerdictCounter-Strike 2 honors the legacy of its predecessor while building the foundation for the future of esports. It preserves the unforgiving skill ceiling I fell in love with as a kid, while delivering the modern upgrades needed for the next decade of competition. Whether you are a casual player or a aspiring pro, this is still the pinnacle of tactical shooters.

text:
Executive Summary

Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) was marketed as a revolutionary leap forward for tactical shooters, built on the Source 2 engine. In reality, it has functioned as a forced, incomplete replacement for Counter-Strike: Global Offensive (CS:GO). By deleting a polished, decade-refined esport and replacing it with a fundamentally flawed product, Valve has downgraded the core gameplay experience, ruined competitive integrity, and alienated its most dedicated player base.
1. Technical Performance & The "Sub-Tick" Illusion

The foundation of any competitive tactical shooter is consistency and visual clarity. CS2 fails catastrophically here.

    The Sub-Tick Failure: Valve replaced traditional 128-tick servers with a proprietary "sub-tick" architecture. While advertised as calculating actions between ticks, the real-world result is severe desynchronization. Players routinely experience dying behind solid walls, delayed kill registration, and rubber-banding. What you see on your screen rarely aligns with what the server decides happened.

    Performance Degradation: The Source 2 engine introduced heavy particle effects (volumetric smoke, molotov fire) that cause massive frame-rate drops, even on high-end hardware. Micro-stutters and 1% low FPS drops make smooth tracking impossible, destroying the fluid movement that CS was famous for.

    The "Michael Jackson" and Animation Bugs: Hitboxes regularly desynchronize from character models, particularly during jumping, crouching, or rapid peeking, turning precise aiming into a lottery.

2. Total Collapse of Anti-Cheat and Competitive Integrity

Competitive matchmaking is entirely unplayable at high levels due to a total lack of effective oversight.

    The Non-Existent Anti-Cheat: Valve’s automated anti-cheat systems (VAC / VACnet) are a laughingstock compared to intrusive kernel-level competitors. Rage-hackers (spin-bots, wallhacks, instant headshots through doors) run rampant in the Premier matchmaking mode without consequence.

    The Third-Party Necessity: The fact that players are forced to download third-party software (like Faceit) just to experience a match free of blatant cheaters is a definitive admission that Valve’s core product is broken.

    Rank Inflation and Matchmaking Chaos: Premier rating points are deeply flawed, routinely matching solo-queue players against coordinated five-stack teams, resulting in stomps rather than competitive games.

3. Disastrous Casual & Community Support

Valve didn't just release a bad game; they actively stripped away content that players loved for years.

    Stolen Content: CS2 launched by deleting half of CS:GO's game modes. Missing maps, the removal of the iconic Arms Race (for months), Retakes, and Danger Zone stripped the game of its variety.

    Murdering the Community Server Browser: The community server browser—the literal lifeblood that birthed Counter-Strike through custom maps, surf, jailbreak, and zombie modes—is clunky, broken, and practically buried. By suffocating the community modding scene, Valve turned a diverse gaming ecosystem into a sterile storefront.

4. Prioritizing Gambling Content Over Gameplay

The development cycle of CS2 heavily implies where Valve’s actual priorities lie.

    Case Simulator with a Side of Shooter: Weapon finishes, knife skins, and cases function perfectly. While netcode, sub-tick fixes, and anti-cheat updates are delayed or ineffective, the steam market and case-opening mechanics remain flawless. The game operates more as a decentralized virtual casino than a competitive tactical esport.

Conclusion: The Final Verdict

Score: 3/10

Counter-Strike 2 is a massive step backward. Valve took a masterpiece of competitive balance (CS:GO), deleted it from existence so players couldn't go back, and forced an unpolished, cheater-infested, sub-tick experiment onto the community. It is a game that does not respect its players' time, competitive drive, or PC hardware.

Until Valve introduces a functional anti-cheat and fixes the desynced netcode, CS2 is an embarrassing regression that is not worth your time.

executive summary

counterstrike 2 cs2 was marketed as a revolutionary leap forward for tactical shooters built on the source 2 engine. in reality it has functioned as a forced incomplete replacement for counterstrike global offensive csgo. by deleting a polished decaderefined esport and replacing it with a fundamentally flawed product valve has downgraded the core gameplay experience ruined competitive integrity and alienated its most dedicated player base.
1. technical performance  the subtick illusion

the foundation of any competitive tactical shooter is consistency and visual clarity. cs2 fails catastrophically here.

    the subtick failure valve replaced traditional 128tick servers with a proprietary subtick architecture. while advertised as calculating actions between ticks the realworld result is severe desynchronization. players routinely experience dying behind solid walls delayed kill registration and rubberbanding. what you see on your screen rarely aligns with what the server decides happened.

    performance degradation the source 2 engine introduced heavy particle effects volumetric smoke molotov fire that cause massive framerate drops even on highend hardware. microstutters and 1 low fps drops make smooth tracking impossible destroying the fluid movement that cs was famous for.

    the michael jackson and animation bugs hitboxes regularly desynchronize from character models particularly during jumping crouching or rapid peeking turning precise aiming into a lottery.

2. total collapse of anticheat and competitive integrity

competitive matchmaking is entirely unplayable at high levels due to a total lack of effective oversight.

    the nonexistent anticheat valves automated anticheat systems vac  vacnet are a laughingstock compared to intrusive kernellevel competitors. ragehackers spinbots wallhacks instant headshots through doors run rampant in the premier matchmaking mode without consequence.

    the thirdparty necessity the fact that players are forced to download thirdparty software like faceit just to experience a match free of blatant cheaters is a definitive admission that valves core product is broken.

    rank inflation and matchmaking chaos premier rating points are deeply flawed routinely matching soloqueue players against coordinated fivestack teams resulting in stomps rather than competitive games.

3. disastrous casual  community support

valve didn't just release a bad game they actively stripped away content that players loved for years.

    stolen content cs2 launched by deleting half of csgo's game modes. missing maps the removal of the iconic arms race for months retakes and danger zone stripped the game of its variety.

    murdering the community server browser the community server browserthe literal lifeblood that birthed counterstrike through custom maps surf jailbreak and zombie modesis clunky broken and practically buried. by suffocating the community modding scene valve turned a diverse gaming ecosystem into a sterile storefront.

4. prioritizing gambling content over gameplay

the development cycle of cs2 heavily implies where valves actual priorities lie.

    case simulator with a side of shooter weapon finishes knife skins and cases function perfectly. while netcode subtick fixes and anticheat updates are delayed or ineffective the steam market and caseopening mechanics remain flawless. the game operates more as a decentralized virtual casino than a competitive tactical esport.

conclusion the final verdict

score 310

until valve introduces a functional anticheat and fixes the desynced netcode cs2 is an embarrassing regression that is not worth your time.

text:
I have spent 633 hours in this game.
I have watched my crosshair tremble over heads I should have hit. I have clutched 1v3s in silence and then immediately thrown the next round by peeking B long like I invented overconfidence. I sit at 8,500 ELO — which sounds respectable until you realise it just means I now lose to people who are precisely as mediocre as I am, but faster.
Counter-Strike 2 is not a game. It is a mirror you fire bullets into.
The gunplay is still the best in the world. Not because it's smooth or forgiving — it isn't — but because it is honest. Your spray goes wide because you pulled the mouse wrong. You lost the duel because you peaked at the wrong tick. The game never lies to you. That's both its gift and its cruelty.
The maps breathe differently than GO. The sound design rewards obsessive listening. The Premier system will make you feel like a genius for six rounds and then remind you, efficiently and without ceremony, that you are not.
At 8.5k, the matches are chess with rifles. Everyone knows the setups. Everyone expects the read. Winning becomes less about mechanics and more about thinking one layer deeper than the guy thinking one layer deeper than you. It is exhausting. I queued again immediately.
I do not know if this game is good for me. I know I'm not done with it.
633 hours in. Still undefeated in my own head.
— 8,500 ELO. Every loss was a fluke.

text:
The Source 2 transition brought reworked visuals and physics-based smokes that genuinely move the game forward; the smokes are so pretty now you almost want to get lost in them. The sub-tick system changed how shooting feels, and thanks to it I'm absolutely certain every death of mine is perfectly fair (it's not). Still, the movement and gunplay kept that classic bone structure, so veterans feel right at home immediately.
On the anti-cheat side, things are better than before now that VACnet has improved, but you'll still bump into the occasional "suspicious" people in Premier. To be honest, I also learned to check my own crosshair placement before blaming a cheater. Despite all that, the competitive depth, economy management, and team play are still the best in the genre.
That said, I genuinely miss CS:GO. There was something about its feel, its cleaner simplicity and that old-school identity, that CS2 hasn't fully recaptured for me. CS:GO was just better in a lot of ways, and a part of me wishes I could go back.
It's free, constantly updated, and has a massive community. You can spend hours trash-talking with your friends, what more could you want? It's a game every FPS fan should try.
Highly recommend, but consider this a warning so your life doesn't get derailed.

text:
Love it or hate it, it's impossible to say Counter Strike is not one of the most important game franchises of all time, which makes the newest iteration in the series, CS2, automatically one of the most important games in the present day. And since it's technically free to play, it's hard to critique in a particularly meaningful way. The hundreds of hours (into the thousands, if you've really got nothing better to do) that one can get out of a game like CS2 can't really be compared with a cheap but short story driven indie game or massive open world triple-A adventure games, because it nestles itself into the corner quadrant of "free to play" and "infinitely replayable."

You see, because it costs no money, there is no reason to play apart from your own enjoyment. You are in no way obligated to play to make the most of a purchase even if it's not [i]exactly[/i] the game you wanted. If you have something better to do, go do it. CS2 is an open bar serving the same drink over and over. If you like it, you'll come for more, and if you don't, you're free to leave without wasting any cash or time. A review seems almost pointless. And yet I will attempt to give my thoughts anyway.

CS2 is fun. Its rules have existed for longer than many of the people playing it. If I had a nickel every time someone in chat during a match or on a forum post asks the question "how do I aim down sights?" I would probably have at least enough money to buy Prime status and develop a crippling addiction to gambling on cases for skins. For a lot of people, this is a good thing (the longevity of rules, not the crippling gambling addiction), but I think this also causes some serious issues when it comes to the fact that Counter Strike as a franchise is incredibly stubborn to change. And understandably so. People who played the original CS as young adults could be playing CS2 in their 50s, and doubtlessly beyond for those lucky enough to live that long. 

But as a result, many people have an expectation of what CS is supposed to be. People get attached to their Dust 2 and their Mirage and their AKs and their M4s and nothing ever changes. That's okay, CS is competitive. Consistency is key. Imagine if CS2 suddenly added aiming down sights and sliding and destructible walls and airdrops and dialogue that exposited the lore behind the game and each map and so on. The game's consistency is it's selling point. And in a competitive sense, it is hyper palatable. You can hop on at any time and have largely the same experience as you would at any other time.

But I think a lot is lost because of this. In the most competitive sense of Counter Strike, only a handful of the many weapons will be used. You could go a thousand matches using the starting pistol, the Desert Eagle, your side's cheapest submachine gun, your side's flagship rifle, and the AWP.  Literally everything else is mostly just incremental stops along the sliding money scale to the next item of the above list of five novel guns. But because these meta picks have existed for so long, and have received so many skins and so much attention and everyone and their mothers know the spray patterns and how to use them, Valve has no incentive to create viable alternatives.

Let's talk about anti cheat. I think it is admirable that Valve refuses to implement kernel level anti cheat. It's also probably a decent business decision given SteamOS is based on Arch, and they're not keen to leave their own hardware out to dry by releasing games that are fundamentally incompatible with its OS. But there are just so many wallhackers that it sometimes feels that any given game is entirely decided by pure random chance whether the enemy team (or yours) has somebody hacking. I find myself spectating certain suspect players for several rounds trying to look for signs of cheating and I often find them. And even for the ones you [i]can[/i] identify as hacking and report, there's no guarantee that anything will ever come of it. You might end up decreasing your trust rating and getting stuck with even more hackers.

This is a neverending fight for any competitive multiplayer game, but it does not help that players are financially incentivized through weekly drops to bot the game with as many accounts as possible. Not to mention the entire skin market, which is an almost entirely unregulated casino and a miniaturized economy where basic changes can literally cause billions of dollars of lost value for those, frankly, who are stupid enough to put their life savings into colorful weapon skins in a video game, and not in a high yield savings account, or an index fund, or literally just gold bullion buried in the forest, all of which will probably give you a better return on investment and, simultaneously, be far less likely to result in you losing literally everything you own.

I've said a lot of negatives about CS2, but I would recommend you give it a try. It's free! I know Valve isn't going to fix any of these gripes, because they make way too much money off skins and legacy players to butcher their cash cow for no reason, but it's still a fun time. Just stay out of the casino in the back. The house always wins.

text:
TLDR: CS2 is a no-no currently, lacks what CSGO had, community's infested with a major population of immature brats, problematics worming their way on both Casual and Comp modes, greed takes priority than the community, devs abandoned the game, and many other 99 problems we could list but am not going to tire myself with that.

Starting with the first and foremost cardinal advice, I recommend against playing Counter-Strike 2 in its current state.

It's the hollow promise that was never realized and also the reason that Counter-Strike Global Offensive deserved better than to be replaced by the game that's now left out to be severely rotten without any meaningful changes. The gameplay is as the same as you would've had it on Global Offensive, except this time it's practically turned into a barebones version that is not feeling the same. 2's Matchmaking modes are lacking where Global Offensive had much more on offer. You only have Casual, Deathmatch, Arms Race and Retakes. That, and the competitive modes such as Premier, Competitive and Wingman that I'm not ever keen on touching with a 10-foot pole because of the horrifyingly inconsistent anti-cheat that allows cheaters to illegitimately rank their way up through unprofessional means.

On Casual matchmaking modes, you'd be lucky to have some coherent individuals that know how to play the game, but chances are there are plenty of rotten apples who will go out of their way to completely sabotage your normal session and just attempt to votekick you out of the lobby because they suck and thrive off their bloated egos, or if you're playing way better than everyone on your team. Just because you happen to play better doesn't mean the immature runts have enough reason to do a votekick over it, yet they do.

Deathmatch? DON'T GET ME STARTED. Every map group has become infested with the botfarm problem that's been plaguing the mode for as long as it's still prevalent, yet nothing is done to outright use digital insecticide to brick the living daylights out of the lowly insect botfarm-operated accounts (with PRIME STATUS, mind you) that continue to worm their way into this mode for every weekly drop they can get.

Arms Race is just plainly stripped down two Gun-Game maps and two Shoots maps. Not worth the effort IMO, since it's just mindless gun-rolling and I don't care much for it.

Retakes is 4v3 straight-shot of the normal defusal mode but it's a genuinely 99% losing and 1% winning because you can't get teammates that constantly run into enemy players or sometimes get lucky if you have a team that aims to win. I even saw a duo of russian brats votekicking a singular teammate off the T side because they want to play duo and yet they lost because of it, that's how inept a harmfully immature majority of players are distinguished from the sane and mentally stable human players.

Like Global Offensive, you can have plenty of skins for your weapons, gloves, even agents to make you look distinguished from the default agents on every map because for some ungodly reason factions had to be removed and put back in as standalone agents that you have to buy. You can already guess that some high-tier skins and items even cost a lotta real world money and it's not feasible to save money on when you got cases that you have to buy untradable keys for.

The game may have stood solid in the beginning of its lifespan, but in the long-standing overtime it's worn out its welcome. No sane person should ever have to endure the majority of caustic dimwits within the playerbase who aim to sabotage your experience no matter the mode by being obnoxious and votekicking you for any reason to boost their bloated ego. And the fact that it's in a sordid state is inexcusable especially when greed takes priority within the high-ups and the devs have left the game and its players on life support.

You can try your hand at finding community servers as you wish if you want to get away from the matchmaking chaos, but it doesn't matter either way. Mileage may vary depending on every sane human person playing Counter-Strike 2 and looking for the community servers to play on.

Counter-Strike Global Offensive deserved better.

text:
Premier matchmaking in CS2 is far too often ruined by blatant cheating and abusive communication.

When the matches are filled with decent people, the game is still excellent. Counter-Strike at its best is one of the most enjoyable competitive games ever made. But in Premier, I am too often placed in matches with obvious spinbot/aimbot behaviour, or with players being severely abusive over voice/text chat. When that happens, the match stops being competitive, enjoyable, or even worth playing.

The worst part is that the system often makes everyone else feel trapped. A player can receive reports from an entire team in the first few rounds, but the rest of the lobby is still stuck waiting for the match to end unless they want to abandon and risk a cooldown. That creates a miserable experience for legitimate players.

VAC and the reporting system currently feel ineffective from the player side. Maybe action is happening behind the scenes, but it does not feel visible, fast, or strong enough to protect the Premier experience.

CS2 can still be brilliant when the match quality is good. But right now, Premier is too inconsistent and too often ruined by cheaters and abusive players. For the main competitive mode of Counter-Strike, that is deeply disappointing.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 — это не просто обновление, а полноценная новая эра для самого культового соревновательного шутера в истории, которая вывела привычный игровой процесс на совершенно другой уровень. Переход на современный движок Source 2 кардинально преобразил игру визуально: графика стала невероятно сочной, текстуры — четкими, а переработанная система освещения сделала карты светлыми, читаемыми и современными. Однако главным триумфом разработчиков стали инновационные «динамичные» дымовые завесы. Эти новые смоки полностью меняют тактический рисунок боя: они реалистично заполняют пространство, реагируют на свет, а главное — их можно временно «развеять» точным взрывом осколочной гранаты или прострелить пулями, чтобы увидеть соперника. Это добавило игровому процессу колоссальной глубины и вариативности, заставляя даже опытных ветеранов переучиваться на ходу.

При всех визуальных и технологических новшествах CS2 сохранила свой главный стержень — кристально честный соревновательный дух. Здесь по-прежнему нет и намека на механику «pay-to-win», а донат влияет исключительно на внешний вид оружия. Исход каждой дуэли и каждого раунда решает только ваш личный скилл: идеальный контроль отдачи, молниеносная реакция, доскональное знание геометрии карт и умение читать действия оппонентов. Экономическая система игры — это отдельный вид искусства, превращающий шутер в глубокую стратегию, где команде приходится постоянно балансировать между рискованными закупками и сейвом денег, просчитывая бюджет врага на два шага вперед.

Новый премьер-режим с открытой таблицей рейтинга вывел соревновательный азарт на предел, мотивируя развиваться, кооперироваться с адекватными тиммейтами и играть на максимуме возможностей. Безусловно, проект до сих пор полируется патчами, античит периодически заставляет понервничать, а в голосовом чате всё так же можно встретить токсичных игроков. Но когда вы заходите в игру сыгранной пятеркой друзей, координируете идеальный выход на плент через сложные раскидки гранат и вырываете победу в тяжелейшем овертайме — игра дарит настолько мощный, чистый прилив адреналина и эндорфинов, который не способна повторить ни одна другая игра на рынке. Это монументальный, глубокий и бесконечно реиграбельный тактический шедевр, который заслуженно удерживает звание главного сетевого шутера планеты.

text:
Has all the basics an FPS player could want.
If you like casual FPS games, play the casual mode. You'll find plenty of good sports at all skill levels looking for the same thing you are. If you like competitive FPS games, play the competitive mode. You'll find plenty of competitive folks who don't have an issue telling you how you could improve.
Those two sides of the community tend to stick to their game modes, so you shouldn't have to worry too much about verbal whiplash from sweats in casual, and you won't find too many weak links queuing for competitive.
The learning curve can take you a hot minute, but you'll find yourself enjoying the slow ride to the top.

text:
[h1]I always used to say "I wish there was another game exactly like CS except it was actually fun"[/h1]

[spoiler]Years later I realized it's literally just going outside and living my life.[/spoiler]

[table][tr][th]I think Ive always circled back somehow, when living life becomes not so fun anymore, CS was always there to take care of this feeling I'd have about lacking in preparation or plans.

But somehow.. since I know nobody can ever be truly perfect at anything. I started sacrificing my body, organs, and mind, to the only thing in my life that was ever capable of letting me think I would eventually be "good enough"; but even then, I dont think it would have ever actually been me. 

My heart can't belong to something that doesn't care about how I feel.

So Counterstrike has always just been a distraction that made me think 
"maybe one day I'll deserve to feel like I'm enough"
rather than being something that was capable of ever making sure I knew that I always had been enough.

And I think that's why this game is so addicting. Because if I just escape everything one more time; maybe this time I will finally feel like I'm doing something right.

When Im playing a match, nothing about my life matters anymore except my ability to perform well.
There is literally not a single moment in a round that I can waste on thinking about anything other than what my next move is about to be.

So everything just disappears and something more rewarding takes its place; but only if I can execute it properly.

I think this can become really dangerous, because it effectively replaces every feeling required for emotional growth with something that makes you only feel positively about practical growth.

It is a drug. It's a drug that teaches you how to commit responsibily, how to work with others, how to see things other people might not, how to abstain from things you desire, how to find motivation in devastation...

But the only thing it will NEVER be able to teach you is how to implement how you truly feel about these things into who you are as a person.[/th][/tr][/table]

Maybe I just miss when it was my innocent escape from highschool rather than my devastating escape from how I feel about myself.

text:
Counter‑Strike 2 finally gets the anti‑cheat system it deserves with VAC 3.0. The new version detects cheats in real time, hands out immediate permanent bans, and continuously learns from emerging threats—so you’re almost never fighting against aimbots or wall‑hacks. The matchmaking feels genuinely fair, and the competitive ladder stays clean and rewarding.

From Tel Aviv to Haifa, the Israeli CS community has embraced the game with open arms. Local tournaments fill up in minutes, Israeli streamers are buzzing, and the “Made in Israel” spirit is evident in every clutch and call‑out. It’s refreshing to see a title where the cheat‑free experience matches the passion of the players—especially when that passion comes from a nation that lives for competitive gaming.

If you want a shooter where every round is decided by skill, teamwork, and strategy—not by cheaters—and you enjoy playing alongside a vibrant Israeli community, CS2 delivers it perfectly. Highly recommended!

text:
I have played and enjoyed counterstrike for over 10 years. This was once my favorite game and I enjoyed collecting skins and watching all the majors. I was never very good (usually high silver or low gold) but I always had fun as a casual player and looked forward to relaxing playing counterstrike with friends after work.

Today I played a competitive match on Office, I was doing ok but not great and we were about halfway through the match when I saw a red message on the screen that said the match was cancelled due to VAC detecting irregular gameplay. I was so happy and excited to finally see an anti cheat in action for counterstrike, but when I got back to my solo lobby I saw i had a 20 hour global cooldown. The only details it gave was VAC detected irregular gameplay and when I go to my steam account, the listed reason for the VAC cooldown is: "Unexpected Error". 

I am extremely disappointed, frustrated, and sad at valve for letting this happen. The only thing worse than having no anticheat or a bad anticheat, is an ai based anticheat that gives out false bans or cooldowns. I have submitted a ticket already but I doubt I will ever hear back from a real person. I have accumulated a significant inventory over the years I have been playing and collecting skins but now I am terrified to ever play again after my 20 hour cooldown expires. After looking into this there are several well known players and streamers who have recently experienced this issue too within the last few months.

I feel really let down to have to give up my favorite game with no choice but I won't play a game giving false cooldowns when I have never cheated in any way. I don't want to risk my inventory or my steam account with so many memories either. If you have an inventory worth any meaningful amount or your steam account has any meaning to you, do not risk playing counterstrike in its current state. Wait for the VAC live system to get to a functional state.

This used to be my favorite game, now I just feel betrayed after pouring so much time and money into it.

0/10, find a different game to play.

text:
[h3]
Honest review:
I loved 1.6 and CS:GO.
But this game is terrible compared to both of those games.[/h3]


[h1]So, whats wrong?..[/h1]
[h3]Colors [/h3]- Washed out, most crosshairs are almost invisible.
[h3]Tracers [/h3]  They now finally represent real bullet trajectory, however, because of the colors, you cant see them in many scenarios.
 [h3]Sub-tick[/h3] - Complete nonsense, that broke the game, everything is out of synchronization - your screen and server doesnt match, neither your enemies, you are fighting ghost.
[h3]Animations[/h3] - Out of synchronization, every game your nades are stuck in your hand, because of subtick and desync.
[h3]Enemies animations[/h3] - Yeah..new animations made it little less annoying, but still, almost non-readable movement of your enemies.
[h3]Bobbing of your weapon[/h3] - you cant set it, still...this game just makes no sense.
[h3]Run and gun [/h3]- everysingle match you are getting one-tapped from enemy, thats clearly on the move, many times, it hapenned in csgo too, but it was rare, this is just a joke at this point.
[h3]Sound [/h3] - omg, how i hate this sound design, everything is muffled, or you feel like you are under water, every match your enemies dont make sound while full running towards you..
[h3]FPS and stability[/h3] - everything in this game is bound to the fps, everyaction you do is affected by the fps, however this game is unoptimized mess. Yes, it is much better than it was on realease, however, this is and was the number one competetive shooter on the world, but you cant compete, when your fps are getting demolished in every gunfight, usage of utility and so on, your 1% fps lows and 0.1% fps are in terrible state, everytime you try to do something.
[h3]Game feels like 60 hz [/h3]- because of desync, subtic, fps drops.
[h3]Input and output lag [/h3]- most of the times your actions are 200-400ms slower than intended, you can feel it happen live while playing.
[h3]Peekers advantage and network [/h3] - no matter how good your ping is, because your game is processed in the higher ping of players fighting. That makes good ping actually worse, because only the things that matter - gunfight is delayed for you, so you are just incosistent always. 
[h3]Hitboxes dont match players movement [/h3] - because of subtick and desync in client/server side, you can clearly see that shots are backtracking, while if you are spot on - you miss. Thats the reason why you are getting killed behind cover via wallbang in killfeed - it doesnt matter that the cover is not possible to wallbang, it just backtracks you and add icon in the killfeed.
[h3]Hitting enemies [/h3] - if you hit someone, you cant "feel" that you are doing something, in csgo you could see and feel that you catched someone, there not anymore.
[h3]Matchmaking [/h3] - elo balancing if you queue with someone without elo is broken, for you and also for enemies.
[h3]Cheaters [/h3] - almost every, if not everygame in the premier has someone cheating, i checked so many demos...
[h3]VAC [/h3]- oblivous.
[h3]State of valve official DM [/h3]- Did you try it?  70% of "players" are bots, that are almost spinbotting, with aimbot turned to 100% and VAC or VALVE doesnt do anything. Its sad that this is missed by VAC, pure ragecheating undetected. Everyone knows and can identify those bots in span of 5 seconds, but valve cant.  We have no option to report it or anything.
[h3]Movement[/h3] - this also feels so bad, just clunky mess, also in desync.
[h3]Binds [/h3]- you cant bind one action to more than one button, like why would you put this away?
[h3]Removed crosshair styles [/h3] - again, why would you remove this? 
[h3]Removed mouse accelartion [/h3] - i know that only few % of players used it, but again, just why?..
[h3]Competetive[/h3] - everymap requires their own rank, but rank does not matter, because you can queue with silver and global together, so why i cant have my rank and whats the point of the ranks, if it doesnt work?
[h3]Overwatch [/h3]- still, we cant even help ourselves to ban those people in mm, because there is this option anymore.
[h3]CSTV[/h3] - still missing, you cant watch your friends games.
[h3]Community servers [/h3] - still not good or working browser.
[h3] Achievements [/h3] - still missing...
[h3]Lack of real updates [/h3] - almost everyupdate is focused on how to make more money from the community, despite not caring about community.
