text:
Counter-Strike 2 is a landmark upgrade to one of the greatest FPS franchises ever made. The move to Source 2 brings stunning visual clarity, responsive hit registration, and sub-tick networking that makes every shot feel genuinely earned. The smoke grenade overhaul alone is a game-changer — dynamic, reactive smokes that interact with bullets and grenades add a whole new layer of tactical depth that veteran players will love.
The Premier mode with its MMR-based matchmaking finally gives competitive play a meaningful ranking ladder, and the map pool stays tight and iconic.
On the Ban System:
Valve's VAC Live system is a step up — bans happen faster than the old wave-ban model, and the new VAC bans are permanent and non-negotiable, which is the right call.
The honest caveat though —
VAC's cheat detection is still inconsistent. Blatant spinbotters sometimes survive multiple reports for weeks, and subtle wall/aim assist cheaters can fly under the radar entirely since VAC relies heavily on behavioral pattern detection rather than real-time monitoring. The Overwatch system being gone doesn't help either.

text:
This game is a far cry from what CS:GO was in the past, but CS:GO wasn't perfect either. It had its own problems, like cheaters and bad voice chat moderation, but it also had a better engine (Source 1), faster movement, and better shot registration. If Valve focused on gameplay before skins and cosmetics, it is entirely possible for CS2 to be amazing with just a few adjustments.
It's frustrating to spray down a player, run behind a wall, and still die because of the 64-tick (subtick) system—it's bull**** and not fun. Visually, the game looks amazing, and I'm running 200+ FPS even with OBS and Firefox open, but they can still do so much better. In CS:GO, movement was indeed way faster, more skillful, and fun to use; here, it feels "noobified," slow, and built for newer players. Of course, new players are what keep a game alive, but this approach backstabs the older fanbase that has been here since 1.6.

The only thing I feel they did somewhat right was the empty magazine reload update, since it forces you to remember not to "Call of Duty reload." Other than that, Valve focuses too much on skins, and it feels like everything else is being thrown away or discarded. To be fair, I only started playing a year and a half before writing this review, so I only played a little bit of CS:GO. Still, GO felt much better—though not by a massive milestone like some people claim.
If they fix the anti-cheat, hit registration, and movement, I think more people will call this their "forever game." I hope Valve fixes these issues and rethinks the future of Counter-Strike altogether. We are not just numbers for your monthly income; we are people, and we should be heard and listened to more often. Without that connection, this game will become just like Overwatch 2: a game past its prime and heading downhill.

text:
Only game that can make you feel like a tactical genius and a broke idiot at the same time. One minute you’re clutching a 1v5 with a perfectly timed AWP shot that would make your ancestors proud, the next you’re staring at your Steam inventory wondering why you just spent $400 on a skin for a gun you only use when you’re drunk. This game isn’t just a shooter — it’s a high-stakes casino where the house always wins and the prize is slightly better looking pixels.
The gameplay is still pure crack. The gunplay feels crisp, the movement is buttery, and there’s nothing quite like hearing “Bomb has been planted” while your heart tries to escape your ribcage. I love the satisfaction of out-aiming some Russian kid who’s been playing since he was in diapers, or perfectly executing a smoke lineup that makes your teammates call you a god. CS2 turned the classic formula into something modern and beautiful… until you remember the real meta isn’t aim — it’s not opening cases.
This game is an absolute money pit. Valve looked at my bank account and said “challenge accepted.” I’ve dropped more money on cases, stickers, and operation passes than I care to admit. Every time I tell myself “this is the last case,” I end up spending another $80 chasing that dream knife that I’ll probably never pull. My inventory is worth more than my car, and the only thing it does is sit there looking pretty while I still get destroyed by default skins. The skin economy is so predatory it makes loot boxes look like charity.
And don’t get me started on the community. You’ve got the toxic gods screaming in voice chat, the guys who play 40 hours a week and still call everyone “bot,” and the occasional god-tier player who makes you question your entire existence. But somehow it’s all worth it when you top-frag and your friends spam “NICE” in chat like you just won the lottery.
CS2 has me in a chokehold. It’s the only game where I can lose $200 in virtual skins and still say “worth it” after one good match. The addiction is real, the dopamine hits are nuclear, and my financial advisor is crying in the corner.

text:
CS2 undeniably captures a pure, thrilling competitive spirit. The mechanical tension, the adrenaline rush of clutching a round, and the drive to improve your tactical skills are exactly what makes this series legendary.

Unfortunately, all of that brilliant gameplay is completely ruined by an unbearable community. Queuing up without a full team is essentially an emotional roulette. Instead of teamwork and basic communication, you are constantly met with toxicity, screaming, and relentless blame.

It makes the entire experience deeply uncomfortable. You end up fighting your own teammates more than the enemy, which completely kills the fun. Unless you have a full lobby of friends to play with in a relaxed atmosphere, it’s simply not worth the stress. Great game, terrible environment.

text:
⭐ 0/10 — The Cheaters Have Better Matchmaking Than Players

Counter-Strike 2 feels less like a competitive shooter and more like a social experiment designed to test how much suffering a player can endure before uninstalling.

The anti-cheat situation is so bad that every match feels like a coin flip between playing Counter-Strike and starring in a live demonstration of "How Walls Work." Trust Factor? Nobody knows what it does anymore. Good players get punished, questionable players get rewarded, and everyone gets thrown into the same digital dumpster fire.

Then there are the servers. Nothing says "premier competitive experience" quite like dying three business days after reaching cover. Every gunfight feels like you're arguing with the server about what actually happened.

And let's talk about communication from Valve. The community reports problems for months, and the response often feels like a mysterious silence broken only by another case release. Bugs survive longer than most esports careers.

As for Gabe Newell, at this point I picture him as a giant greedy fox sitting on a mountain of case keys, occasionally peeking down at the player base and saying, "Have you considered opening another crate instead of fixing the game?"

CS2 somehow took one of the greatest competitive games ever made and turned it into a trust-factor lottery running on servers powered by wishful thinking.

Recommended? Only if your hobbies include packet loss, existential despair, and trying to determine whether your opponent has incredible game sense or just a second monitor showing the entire map.

Counter-Strike 2 is truly a masterpiece of modern gaming. There’s honestly nothing more satisfying than loading into Premier, winning a few rounds, and then getting instantly headshotted through three walls by a level 2 account named “xXPutinLover69Xx.” The adrenaline rush of losing 400 ELO to someone spinning at Mach 5 while VAC quietly observes in spiritual silence is an experience no other game can replicate.

Speaking of VAC — wow. What an incredible anti-cheat system. I especially appreciate how it gives cheaters enough time to fully explore their creativity before considering any action. Why ban them immediately when they can ruin hundreds of matches first? Truly player-first design philosophy.

One of my favorite aspects of CS2 is the cultural enrichment. Before playing, my Russian vocabulary was limited to “vodka.” Now, thanks to my incredibly friendly teammates, I can confidently recognize phrases like “Cyka Blyat,” “idi nahui,” and several other beautiful expressions screamed directly into my ears at 140 decibels after losing pistol round. It’s basically free language lessons.

The matchmaking experience is also second to none. Every game feels like a social experiment. Will your teammates communicate? Will they buy rifles? Will they understand what a bombsite is? Nobody knows. Sometimes I get paired with tactical masterminds who rush mid with a Nova every single round while blasting music through their microphone. The unpredictability keeps the game fresh.

And let’s not forget the community. Nothing builds character faster than being told to uninstall by someone who finished the game 4-19 while permanently flashbanging the team. The emotional growth I’ve experienced from these interactions has been invaluable.

Performance-wise, CS2 runs amazingly too. I especially enjoy how every update introduces exciting new FPS drops and mysterious stutters. It’s like opening a loot box, except the reward is input lag.

Overall, Counter-Strike 2 is more than just a game. It’s a test of patience, sanity, and emotional stability. Whether you’re losing ELO to blatant cheaters, learning Russian profanity from your teammates, or questioning your life choices after queueing “just one more game” at 2AM, CS2 always delivers a memorable experience.

10/10 — would get wallbanged by a fresh account again.

text:
I absolutely love this game The graphics are incredible especially compared to the old CS which was way too dark. Visually, it's a massive step up and feels great to play.
However, a few things really need some tuning like The economy feels a bit off sometimes. Why should a $5000 gun feel less effective than a $1600 weapon that deals better damage? It doesn't make much sense and The focus on skins, cases, and what is basically gambling can get a bit too much and feels unhealthy for the game's environment but The biggest issue is the toxicity,
There is a massive lack of teamwork. If teammates don't like you, they’d rather watch you lose than help you. On top of that, playing from Saudi Arabia means dealing with serious toxicity and i don't know why. I constantly encounter players (like some Iranian players) who start hurling insults or trying to vote-kick me the moment they realize where I’m from. Even worse, some toxic Arabic-speaking players will absolutely lose their minds and start toxic raging the second you make a single mistake or don't play exactly how they want.
It’s a fantastic game visually and mechanically, but the toxic community and some balance issues really hold it back

text:
Counter-Strike 2 is actually a pretty fun game… when you’re not getting headshotted through three walls by a guy named “TotallyLegitPlayer”.

The gunplay feels smooth, the graphics look great, and every match has that perfect mix of strategy and chaos. One second you’re pulling off a tactical masterpiece, the next your teammate is flashbanging the entire squad including himself.

Sadly, the biggest boss in the game isn’t the enemy team. Some players have better wall vision than my Wi-Fi router. I swear one guy knew my location before I even loaded into the map.

CS2 can be incredibly addictive and hilarious with friends. Nothing builds friendships faster than screaming “HE’S ONE HP!” while spectating your teammate miss an entire magazine.

Overall:
Great game.
Great mechanics.
Great memes.
Questionable humanity.

8/10 — would get destroyed by suspiciously talented Russians again.

text: As a new player who isn't very good at the game, the only mode I can play is deathmatch because people kick me out instantly in any other mode, and I don't have any friends to play with to vote against it for me. I can't improve much because playing against bots isn't very good practice; bots behave very differently from real players. Even in deathmatch, there are tons of case-farming bot accounts that kick real players out instantly. There are also lots of people using cheats and multiple accounts, who kick players out the instant they join. Then when you get kicked out and try to join another server, you can't play at all because Valve always tries to put you in the server you just got kicked from, and then after wasting your time waiting for it to load, you get an error message saying you can't join the server. Bots, cheaters, and anyone who wants to, can literally prevent you from playing the game at all, until the server they kick you from closes. Another big issue is that there are so many toxic players in this game who will hurl slurs at you just for being inexperienced, or for no reason at all. I had to turn off the chat because of this. Reporting does nothing. Valve, as a multi-billion dollar company, you can do better. You should be investing in some decent, real human moderation instead of donating 20 million dollars to AI slop. You should be banning bots, cheaters, and those who harass and bully others. But you won't because you only care about money. The option to vote players out should be removed, especially in deathmatch. It is way too prone to abuse and prevents new players like me from getting better at the game. Some people would respond to that with "What about cheaters?". The answer is that this game wouldn't have so many cheaters if Valve actually had decent moderation. Innocent players should not be punished just because Valve has terrible moderation. At this point, I'm only playing this game to get some Steam credit from item drops, as I don't have much money, and I want to save up for some games to take my mind off of things and hopefully play with some friends someday. This game could be fun with some friends, but as an inexperienced player trying to play by myself, it's really not enjoyable.

text:
If you're looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if you're looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.

Ingredients:

1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter
2. 1/2 cup white sugar
3. 1/2 cup brown sugar
4. 1/4 cup water
5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon
6. 1 pinch salt
7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced
8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie

Directions:

1. Preheat oven to 425 degrees F (220 degrees C).
2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside.
3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices.
4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal.
5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust.
6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C), and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 is still one of the most fun and competitive FPS games ever made. The gunplay is great, the maps are iconic, and there really isn't another game that scratches the same itch. When you get a good match, it's easy to see why people have been playing Counter-Strike for over 20 years.

The biggest problem is the cheating situation. VAC feels like it only works when someone is blatantly rage hacking and making it obvious. Meanwhile, there are plenty of players, including Prime accounts, who try to hide their cheats and make them look legitimate. Anyone who has spent enough time in Premier or Competitive knows exactly what I'm talking about.

What's frustrating is that Valve is a billion-dollar company with more than enough resources to improve anti-cheat, yet it often feels like the issue isn't a priority. Legitimate players are left questioning whether someone is just having a good game or quietly using assistance that never gets detected.

CS2 is still a great game at its core, but it's sad to see it in its current state. The potential is there, the gameplay is there, but the cheating problem continues to hold it back.

cs2 is still a great game at its core but it's sad to see it in its current state. the potential is there the gameplay is there but the cheating problem continues to hold it back.

text:
In my opinion, Valve has lost interest in CS2.

While Deadlock receives major gameplay updates, reworks, new mechanics, and constant developer attention, CS2 feels abandoned.

The cheating problem continues to frustrate players, trust in VAC is at an all-time low, and many long-time fans feel ignored.

What makes it even worse is the growing feeling that Valve cares more about monetization than about the actual game. Some players believe that Valve would prefer more skin trading to happen directly through the Steam Market, where they collect fees, rather than through third-party marketplaces. Whether that's true or not, the lack of communication and focus on core issues has created a lot of distrust within the community.

Counter-Strike used to be Valve's flagship competitive title. Today it feels like the game exists to generate revenue while anti-cheat, content updates, and player concerns take a back seat.

Meanwhile, Deadlock gets the attention, innovation, and development effort that CS2 players have been asking for.

text:
[h1]CS2 WILL WASTE YOUR TIME[/h1]

[h2]THE LEGACY[/h2]
Counter Strike... The name itself evokes a particular feeling depending on who you are. If you've never touched the game and want to begin, the name won't end up living up to the legacy you've heard about.

1.6, source, csgo... These games are what made Counter Strike the sport it is today.

[b]Cs2 on the other hand is a state of a game![/b]

[h2]The Cheater Problem[/h2]
You WILL absolutely find a cheater almost every match. You have a greater chance they are on the enemy team as you occupy one of the 5 players on your side. So yes, the game will actually be rigged against you. Plenty of players cheat subtly, and a decent portion blatantly cheat without shame shooting you through smokes, aim botting, and wall hacking. These cheaters have been at it for years and no sign of any bans. Every so often you might hear of a purge, VAC bans hitting thousands of accounts at once, but don't be fooled. They will be back immediately.

It get's even worse! [b]Cheats are made to make people rich![/b]

The deathmatch servers are ridden with automated bot accounts playing with cheats to farm skins. The economy of the CS market has attracted leeches. If you engage with any trading system online a scammer will try to get in touch with you at least once a day. The greed that encompasses this game actually gives me a sickening feeling in my gut. It's disgusting.

[h2]Why Is The Game Still Popular?[/h2]
[i]Cs2 is hated by the majority but still popular[/i]

Despite the cheating problem many people still play the game... Why?

Either they're addicted to the skins, the gambling, or elo grinding. 

Or they simply fell in love with what counter strike is, but I assure you even they aren't happy with Cs2. It's sounds are off, the movement is sluggish and floaty in comparison to the beloved CSGO (giving the offensive a gigantic advantage), grenades throw late and far too slow, alias binds and optimisation has been removed from the game, the list goes on. No one likes the state of Cs2, not even the pros.

[h2]There Is No Placement For New Players[/h2]
The skill ceiling is so gigantically high and the matchmaking ranks are terribly designed that you'll have a terrible experience working for an accurate rank. With 1.2k hours in this game I am still considered the lowest rank, and I have spent at least a third of this time grinding out training and watching demos back. 

I have learned crosshair placement, counter strafing, angles, map timing, utility line ups, spray control patterns, spray transfers, tap shooting, burst shooting, crab walking, and plenty of strategies. Despite all I have learned I often bottom frag, losing duels to worse players who can run and shoot with a rifle worrying little about their spray and accuracy. 

I can't explain it but it actually feels like RNG about 50% of the time. If you have a bad game, it's not likely going to get better, you'll feel as if some players have a luck on their side whilst you have none.

[h2]Is My Complaints Valid?[/h2]
Maybe all of this sounds like a mental issue? Yes and no. You need to fight confidently for a good chance at winning, a bad mindset can literally lose you the entire game and downgrade the performance of your team mates. The reason why I partially say no is because I have walked into this game confident, with a healthy mindset and relentlessly kept it despite having such a rough time. But to no avail I can lose so hard even with confidence in my abilities, and consistently lose game after game.

You could sink thousands of hours into this game and get utterly nowhere, remain in the worse rank, have terrible stats and nothing to show for your work because this game favours spray and praying, and it also has a terrible ranking system meaning that decent players with over 3 thousand hours could be in your low rank. In fact it's common that even in the low elo ranks you'll die the millisecond your tiny head appears on the enemy screen, it doesn't matter how fast you're moving or if you learned to stop pressing "w" as you strafe.

Anyone who knows this game reading my review might see this is "skill issue" and they'd be right. I'm terrible at this game, bottom of the barrel. And it's not uncommon for people like me with 1k hours to be this bad, I'd say the majority of players with this amount of time are this bad. The game is so unbelievably hard. That would be fine if there was something worthwhile for the time and effort. But there isn't.

If you're on a losing streak you will absolutely feel like breaking something. The game recognises your losing streak and decides to take away 500 elo per loss and give back 100 per win due to this. It's designed to throw you so far back so quickly and destroy everything you've worked so hard for. It could have taken 500 hours to learn and improve to properly rank up, and in hours lose it all.

I've had weeks where every game all I got was a 4 v 5 team due to a leaver, a blatant enemy cheater, or a team mate who has never even touched a shooter before. And it's not uncommon to experience this.

[h2]In Summary[/h2]
I love counter strike, but I hate Cs2. I even hate Faceit as this seems to be only slightly better but far more toxic and mentally draining. I could sink in more hours to get better but my time and effort is better put elsewhere. At minimum you want about 2k hours before you could even learn the "basics" of this game and become a reliable player with consistent performance.

If you want to give this game a go, by all means go for it. But you will have many REAL LIFE days ruined literally due to a video game. If you're naturally gifted mechanically at shooters on the mouse and keyboard, maybe you'll have a better experience, I have friends that have had a much better and smoother experience due to their gifts, and have swiftly put in 8k hours (I am not saying they haven't had to struggle to get where they have got to, but their gifts have undoubtedly persuaded them to continue playing after 1k hours). I on the other hand personally can't bare another 1 thousand hours to just be called "average", or worse yet still be considered "bad" by the majority of players.

If you like competitive games but can't handle the mental strain of terrible ranking systems, unfortunately I think you're out of luck, no game seems to have it. CS2 being one of the worst I've seen.

If you came to read negative reviews because you're mad. Hi, turn from competitive games, it only hurts your pride, seek Jesus instead, He will give more than this game will ever have to offer. Love to all. If you do decide to play this game, good luck and I truly hope you have a better experience than me.

CSGO gets a 8/10
CS2 gets a 3/10

text:
I downloaded this game expecting a “tactical FPS experience.”

5 minutes in, I realized I had actually installed:

A financial simulator where the economy system teaches you to never save money because everyone ignores it anyway

A psychological experiment disguised as competitive gaming

A global communication test where my team consists of:

one player with no mic,

one player with a mic permanently stuck in “live podcast mode,”

and one guy playing on WiFi powered by a microwave from another dimension



Meanwhile, the enemy team is made of:

a 12-year-old who moves at Mach 5,

headshots through walls, smoke, time, space, and my will to live,

and someone named “tax fraud” who always goes 47-2 for absolutely no reason


The economy system is simple: “Save your money.” My team: no.

So we buy pistols, rush, and die like we’re part of a documentary about bad decisions.

Every match feels like a social experiment funded by Valve to test how much chaos 5 strangers can handle before mentally disconnecting.

Graphics are amazing though — I can clearly see the exact pixel where my hope disappears after every one-tap headshot.

10/10
Uninstalled it three times this week, reinstalled it four times.
Clearly I have unresolved issues 👍

text:
Gameplay Experience

The biggest reason I liked CS2 is its tight and realistic gameplay mechanics. Unlike many fast-paced arcade shooters, CS2 requires patience, teamwork, map knowledge, and precision aiming. Every bullet matters, and winning a clutch round feels extremely satisfying.

The movement and shooting mechanics feel smooth and polished. Learning recoil patterns, utility usage, and positioning made the game more interesting over time. Even after many matches, the gameplay never felt repetitive because every opponent plays differently.

Graphics and Visual Improvements

Compared to older Counter-Strike versions, CS2 looks much better. The upgraded graphics, lighting, smoke effects, and map redesigns make the game feel modern while still keeping the classic Counter-Strike identity.

The new smoke grenade physics are one of my favorite additions. Smokes react dynamically with bullets and grenades, which adds more tactical depth during matches. Maps like Dust II, Mirage, and Inferno look cleaner and more detailed than before.

Competitive Nature

One of the strongest aspects of CS2 is its competitive environment. Winning matches depends heavily on communication and teamwork. I liked how every player has an important role, whether entry fragging, supporting teammates, or clutching rounds.

The ranking system motivates players to improve constantly. Losing can feel frustrating sometimes, but improving aim and strategy over time feels very rewarding. This competitive pressure is what keeps the game exciting.

Weapons and Sound Design

The weapon mechanics are excellent. Guns like the AK-47, AWP, and M4A1-S feel powerful and satisfying to use. Headshots are extremely rewarding, especially because the game has a high skill ceiling.

The sound design is also impressive. Footsteps, reload sounds, grenade throws, and gunshots help players gather information and react strategically. Good audio awareness can completely change the outcome of a round.

Multiplayer and Team Experience

I enjoyed CS2 even more when playing with friends. Coordinating strategies, making callouts, and celebrating wins together created memorable moments. The teamwork aspect makes the game socially engaging and fun.

However, solo queue can sometimes be frustrating because toxic players and poor communication occasionally ruin the experience. Despite this, good matches easily outweigh the bad ones.

Challenges and Frustrations

Although I liked the game a lot, CS2 is not perfect. Beginners may struggle because the learning curve is very steep. New players often face experienced opponents, which can feel difficult initially.

There are also occasional issues with hackers, matchmaking balance, and server performance. These problems can negatively affect gameplay, especially in competitive matches.

Still, the core gameplay is so strong that these issues did not stop me from enjoying the game overall.

Overall Opinion

Overall, I liked CS2 a lot because it combines:

Competitive gameplay
Tactical teamwork
High skill expression
Smooth shooting mechanics
Strong replay value

text:
Dear Gabe Newell, stop sending your undercover agents into my lobby. I am here to play a competitive tactical shooter, not to run an online orphanage for your mute, lagging, 4-digit-ping tourists who can’t get more than 8 kills in 30 rounds. If I wanted to carry dead weight on my back for 2 hours straight, I’d go work as a mover, not boot up CS2.

The worst matchmaking system in the history of competitive gaming. Valve has officially turned their competitive modes into a lazy casino and a hidden psychological experiment on solo players.
Your personal skill means absolutely nothing here. You can practice your aim, learn utility, clutch your brains out on overtimes, and drop 32 kills with a 1.45 KD — the algorithm doesn't care. It operates on a rotten scheme: if you play well, you get punished for your own skill. The game simply takes your high hidden MMR and uses it as a band-aid to fix lobby balancing for trash players who would otherwise uninstall the game.
You will be consistently, back-to-back, forced to carry dead weight — mute "tourists" with 4-digit ping and a pathetic 8/20 stat line who join Premiere with their e-partners just to flirt in a private Discord call. They will remain silent for 20 rounds, ruining your economy and positioning, mocking you in voice chat when you fail a 1v3 clutch, all while you are breaking your back carrying them. And to top it off, they will mass-report you for toxicity out of sheer helplessness and jealousy. Valve's system will blindly eat those reports, tanking your hidden trust factor and burying you even deeper into the swamp with the next batch of brain-dead bots.
To rank up here as a solo queue player, you have to grind through 100+ matches of pure burnout, because half of your games are pre-determined losses scripted by the system before you even accept the match. The developers actively choke skilled players to enforce an artificial 50% win rate, stretching your progress infinitely just to keep you hooked on the grid and buying their cases. Why would they fix the anti-cheat or build a fair system when digital pixels bring them billions every single night?
If you want competitive hype, transparent Elo, and actual discipline — run to Faceit. Premiere mode in CS2 is an absolute, unadulterated joke. 0/10 - Casino. 

Fix your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matchmaking or go fk yourself with your spineless cases, you greedy bastard.**

text:
Laadisin CS2 alla mõttega, et mängin paar mängu sõpradega õhtuti. “Lihtsalt natuke.” See oli kõige suurem vale, mida ma endale kunagi rääkinud olen.

Alguses oli kõik süütu. Õppisin smoke’e, sain paar head headshot’i ja tundsin ennast nagu järgmine pro mängija. Siis tulid rankid. Ütlesin endale: “Üks mäng veel, et rank tagasi saada.” Sellest sai “üks mäng veel”, siis “üks võit veel”, siis “ma ei saa kaotusega lõpetada.”

Päevad muutusid nädalateks. Ma ei vaadanud enam kella — kell vaatas mind. Uni muutus ettepanekuks, mitte kohustuseks. Kuulsin flashbangi heli isegi siis, kui mäng kinni oli. Iga nurga taga nägin potentsiaalset peek’i.

Ma hakkasin päriselus inimesi hindama selle järgi, kas nad trade’iksid mind või mitte. Kui keegi liiga aeglaselt uksest sisse läks, mõtlesin automaatselt: “bro liigub nagu 300 pingiga.”

Kõige hullem polnud isegi kaotamine. Kõige hullem oli lootus. Lootus, et järgmine mäng on parem. Lootus, et järgmine tiim räägib. Lootus, et seekord keegi ei lähe AFK. Lootus, et seekord universum ei otsusta mulle nelja inimest anda, kes avasid mängu esimest korda oma elus.

Täna olen ma teine inimene. Ma tean rohkem smoke’e kui päris elu oskusi. Mu emotsioonid sõltuvad sellest, kas keegi vajutas õigel ajal defuse’i. Mu päeva kvaliteeti määrab see, kas keegi karjus mikrisse vene keeles pärast pistol round’i.

text:
I've spent 142 days of my life on this game. I'm at peace with that.

3,500 hours buys you specific things: throwing a smoke that lands on the pixel; hearing the first footstep and knowing  which agent skin they paid £15 for; recognising from the opening AK shot whether the player ahead of you has 50 hours or 5,000.

  A few things I now believe:

  - The best CS players aren't the fastest, they're the calmest. Anyone can flick. Almost nobody can hold an angle for
  45 seconds without flinching.
  - Every player has 60ms ping until they die, at which point they had 200ms.
  - "Drop me" is spoken in this game more than any voice line Valve has ever written.
  - A good teammate is the rarest cosmetic in the game.

  Counter-Strike is craft. Most of the time it doesn't love you back. Then it does, for one round, and that pays the
  bill for the next six months.

  Recommended. Obviously.

text: Key IngredientsFlour: 2 ½ cups (300g) all-purpose or self-raisingSugar: 1 ½ cups (300g) granulated or caster sugarButter: ½ cup (113g) unsalted, softened at room temperatureEggs: 4 large, room temperatureLeavening: 1 Tablespoon baking powder (or 1 tsp if using self-raising flour)Flavor & Liquid: 1 Tablespoon vanilla extract and 1 ¼ cups (300ml) milk or buttermilkSalt: ½ teaspoon. Step-by-Step InstructionsPreheat & Prep: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C / Gas Mark 4). Grease two 8-inch or 9-inch round cake tins and line the bottoms with parchment paper.Cream Butter & Sugar: In a large bowl, beat the softened butter and sugar with an electric mixer for 3 to 4 minutes until the mixture is pale, fluffy, and holds air.Add Wet Ingredients: Beat in the eggs one at a time, incorporating well after each, and add the vanilla extract.Mix Dry & Wet: In a separate bowl, whisk together the flour, baking powder, and salt. Add the flour mixture to your wet ingredients in 3 batches, alternating with the milk. Mix on a low speed just until combined to avoid a dense cake.Bake: Pour the batter evenly into your prepared tins. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes, or until a toothpick or wooden skewer inserted into the center comes out clean.Cool: Let the cakes cool in their tins for about 10 minutes, then invert them onto a wire cooling rack to cool completely before frosting.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 has one of the best competitive FPS experiences ever created. The gunplay, movement, clutch moments, and tactical teamwork make the game truly addictive. Every match feels intense and rewarding when played fairly.

But the cheating problem is still damaging the experience for many players. Legit players should not have to question every death or every perfect prefire. Valve really needs stronger anti-cheat solutions and faster detection systems to protect the competitive integrity of CS2.

The game has massive potential — it just needs a cleaner environment for real players to enjoy it.

text:
[h1]COUNTER-STRIKE 2: THE BEAUTY AND THE BREAKING POINT[/h1]

[hr][/hr]
[h2]OVERVIEW:[/h2]
Every match feels like a test of aim, patience and composure. Sometimes it delivers unforgettable teamwork. Sometimes it delivers some of the most toxic people you will ever meet online. Somehow, you queue again anyway.

[hr][/hr]
[h3]GRAPHICS — ★★★★☆[/h3]
Cleaner visuals and sharper lighting modernize the classic maps without losing their identity.

[h3]GAMEPLAY — ★★★★★[/h3]
Simple on paper, brutally deep in practice. Every bullet and every mistake matters.

[h3]COMPETITIVE EXPERIENCE — ★★★★☆[/h3]
Winning feels incredible because nothing comes easy. Clutches create stories you remember for weeks.

[h3]MULTIPLAYER — ★★★☆☆[/h3]
Some teammates restore your faith in online gaming. Others test your patience immediately.

[h3]REPLAYABILITY — ★★★★★[/h3]
The chase for cleaner aim, smarter positioning and better rank progression never really ends.

[h3]SOUND DESIGN — ★★★★★[/h3]
Footsteps, reloads and gunshots carry weight. Audio awareness is half the battle.

[hr][/hr]
[h2]THE BREAKDOWN:[/h2]

[h3]THE ART OF PRECISION:[/h3]

CS2 strips shooters down to their fundamentals. No superpowers, no second chances and no room for panic. Every peek matters. Every smoke can change the round. Landing a perfect headshot after holding an angle for twenty seconds feels more rewarding than most games’ entire progression systems.

[h3]THE COMPETITIVE LOOP:[/h3]

This is where the game shines brightest and hurts the hardest. Winning with a coordinated team feels electric. Tight comebacks and overtime victories create the kind of adrenaline few multiplayer games can match. But losing streaks can feel personal, especially when teammates start blaming each other instead of focusing on the game.

[h3]THE TOXICITY PROBLEM:[/h3]

There is no clean way to say it. Counter-Strike has one of the roughest player communities in competitive gaming. Ego, trash talk and frustration spill into voice chat constantly. Some players treat every mistake like a personal insult. A bad match can feel exhausting.

But strangely, that same environment makes the genuinely good teammates stand out even more. The calm player giving useful callouts, the stranger dropping you a rifle after a bad half, the teammate saying “nice try” after a failed clutch — those moments stay with you.

[h3]MAPS AND ATMOSPHERE:[/h3]

Dust II, Mirage and Inferno are practically gaming landmarks at this point. Learning grenade lineups, timings and rotations slowly transforms chaos into strategy. The maps reward knowledge as much as raw aim.

[h3]THE ADDICTION OF IMPROVEMENT:[/h3]

CS2 keeps pulling you back because there is always something to refine. Better crosshair placement. Faster reactions. Smarter utility usage. Even after hundreds of matches, you still notice mistakes you made two rounds too late. Improvement becomes part of the experience.

[h3]SKINS AND STATUS:[/h3]

Skins are cosmetic, but they became part of Counter-Strike culture long ago. A clean knife animation or a rare weapon finish adds personality to every match. Some inventories look like digital museums at this point.

[hr][/hr]
[h2]FINAL THOUGHTS:[/h2]

Counter-Strike 2 is intense, rewarding and occasionally unbearable. The highs are unforgettable. The lows make you question why you queued in the first place. Yet every perfectly timed flash, every impossible clutch and every coordinated retake keeps pulling you back into another match.

[b]CS2 is not always fun.[/b]
[b]But it is almost always exciting.[/b]

text:
Yes, I'd recommend Counter-Strike 2.
124 hours in and this game is pure love and catastrophe at the same time.
It's the best free Russian language learning program on the planet. You'll somehow pick up Russian, Polish, Turkish, and random Indian accents without even trying. One minute you're just trying to plant the bomb, the next you're understanding slurs in four different languages. The voice chat is basically free immersion.
The gunplay still feels incredible, the rounds are tense as hell, and there's nothing like that dopamine hit when you clutch a 1v4. But it will also tilt you into the shadow realm and make you question humanity.
CS2 is a beautiful disaster. I hate it. I love it. I can't stop playing it.
10/10 would get called racial slurs in broken English again.

text:
Premier and Competitive are infested with skids that use wallhack. Randomly shooting through walls despite it not being common wallbang lineups, shooting perfectly through nades, prefiring corners and sometimes even aimbot. In Deathmatch there's dozens of spinbotters headshotting through the map, and of course there's the bot problem: "players" that maintain the same exact shooting rate with the AK, who, if you spectate will immediately stop all movement and freeze as if they know that you're watching, or even will go to the same spot to knife and be knifed to farm cases (most common on the Italy map for some reason).

The movement and mechanics are great, and is one of the reasons that CS2 has such a massive playerbase. It's simple, yet extraordinarily difficult to master, great for a competitive scene. But if Valve refuses to up their game with VAC detection, whether that be via stricter verification or other measures, then they've effectively made it hostile and unwelcoming towards not only newer players, but veterans. 

I consider myself to be a semi-competitive player, who only plays Premier every now and then. I'd play it more if the people in those matches were actually legit and, if they are, actually were respectful instead of screaming into the mic like a banshee because you didn't hit that blind headshot with the scout.

Whether you feel that this is an acceptable state for the game to be in is for you to judge. But for me, it's a sorry excuse for an FPS when you have to resort to third-party servers like Faceit to have a clean game (which isn't available on Linux). This is a hard thumbs down for me.

text:
Valve is continuing to gouge the player base for more money with every update while failing to give us any reason to spend our money on the game. Between terminal pricing and the new sticker pricing, it is very clear the only plans left for this game are to extract every dollar possible and dump it into Deadlock development. Most of the patches lately have been things that the game should not have launched without, such as Animgraph 2 and the spray update, and the Cache update was extremely underwhelming - just got the map alone with nothing else. Train was a great map that got pulled from prem shortly after updates, which is a very strange decision, and it was replaced by the same map they pulled for it before. I am very convinced the CS2 dev team is two monkeys in a broom closet. 

Things could have been better to deal with if we had real community servers to play on while the mainline game was slow to update, but community servers got nuked on CS2 launch and we are only now starting to see any improvements in that department. We barely even have a server browser, and only recently did we get the community scripting update. The variety in community servers that we saw in the old days has been long gone for years because Valve can't sell us skins on a community server, so they don't care to develop features that benefit them anymore.

I hope you enjoy your new superyacht GabeN, maybe the new $1000 gold Cologne 2026 stickers can cover some maintenance costs for you, can you pick up the diamond studded phone resting upon your solid 24k gold conference table and call the two developers in the CS department to get us some real gameplay updates pretty please?

text:
**Counter-Strike 2 Review: The Same Game, But Somehow More Broken**  

Ah, Counter-Strike 2—the highly anticipated sequel where Valve took everything you loved about CS:GO and said, "What if we made it worse?" The graphics got a facelift, sure, but now your GPU melts faster than your morale when you get one-tapped by a guy named "xX_SniperGod_Xx" for the tenth time in a row.  

The "new" smoke physics are revolutionary—if by revolutionary you mean they now obscure vision in ways that defy both logic and your will to live. And let’s not forget the matchmaking system, which somehow pairs you against literal esports pros while your teammates are still figuring out which key shoots the gun.  

But hey, at least the hit registration is… still questionable! Nothing says "competitive shooter" like dying behind a wall because the servers are powered by a hamster wheel. CS2 isn’t just a game—it’s a masterclass in how to take a decade-old formula, tweak it just enough to call it "new," and still manage to make it feel like a downgrade.  

Final verdict: 5/10, because at least it’s still Counter-Strike… barely.

They say "Leave the game to its demons" which perfectly encapsulates this one. Unless you want to drive your sanity and mental health to complete ruins, I wouldn't recommend even touching CS2.
Which leads me into further analysis of competitive free-to-play games. Take Valorant, Apex Legends, PUBG, Fortnite, Overwatch, CS2, ANY GAME as an example, and question yourself "Why do people keep playing them even though they don't get any satisfaction at the end of it?"
CS2 is a roller-coaster of emotions. One moment you indulge in complete euphoria from getting those juicy one-taps from the AK or DEagle. Then the next moment you're raging about not being able to hit a dude because he keeps on jittering around like a caffeine-drugged junkie.
The game constantly puts you up against stronger opponents whenever you win and against weaker whenever you lose, keeping you just at the edge of not quitting and not being bored. Thus, "Love-Hate relationship" begins to emerge between you and the game: you are left sucked up and beaten, promising yourself to stop playing this, but you're already way too deep into it; you're on a NEURAL level addicted to it - your brain already strung up a connection between getting a kill and releasing dopamine.
INSTANT GRATIFICATION of sorts. It's like jerking off or doomscrolling, i.e looking for a way to make yourself feel better, or chase after dopamine hit. In the end, you're left with nothing but a burned up image of yourself and a ♥♥♥♥ ton of wasted time because winning a game doesn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matter.
Ok I'm done rambling about ts.
This day marks the day that I leave the cuckchair.
♥♥♥♥ this ♥♥♥♥. I'm out.

text: Honestly, CS2 has been one of the most frustrating gaming experiences I’ve had in a long time. The game looks nice visually, but almost everything else feels worse. Hit registration is completely inconsistent. There are so many moments where you clearly hit headshots and nothing happens. Sometimes you spray directly into someone and deal almost no damage, while on your screen the shots are perfectly on target. The hitboxes feel broken, especially during  peeking. Gunfights often feel random instead of skill-based. Matchmaking is another disaster. Almost every game is unbalanced. You either get brand new players on your team who don’t even understand basic utility usage, or you play against obvious smurfs with thousands of hours destroying everyone. There’s barely any middle ground, so competitive matches rarely feel fair. Performance is also terrible. FPS drops, stuttering, random freezes, input lag — all of this still exists even after multiple updates. Sometimes the game feels smooth, and the next match feels completely different for no reason. Sub-tick was supposed to improve responsiveness, but instead the game often feels delayed and inconsistent. Cheaters are still everywhere, especially in Premier mode. Reporting barely seems to do anything, and legit players are the ones suffering because of it. At this point, CS2 feels more polished visually than mechanically. The core gameplay shooting, movement, matchmaking — is simply not reliable enough for a competitive shooter. Right now the game is more annoying than enjoyable.

text:
The core loop hasn't really changed in decades because it just works: you buy a gun, hold an angle, throw a flashbang that inevitably blinds your own teammate, and try to plant or defuse a bomb. When everything clicks and your team is actually communicating, the adrenaline hit is unmatched.

Yeah, you’re going to run into hackers. It’s Counter-Strike, it’s practically part of the heritage at this point. You’ll get that one guy spin-botting or pulling off cross-map wallbangs that make you want to alt-f4.

 But honestly? They don't ruin the game all the time. Most matches are just regular people sweating, missing easy spray patterns, and screaming "purple, what are you doing?" into their mics.

Overall, it’s a great game. If you have the patience to learn the recoil maps and can laugh off the occasional toxic lobby or cheater, it’s easily one of the best competitive experiences out there.

text:
I downloaded the game and purchased the “Premier” subscription.
I want to say that this game is full of cheaters and when I say full of cheaters, I mean more than 80% of the game’s community.
The developers and programmers are struggling to keep up with the growing number of hackers.
In fact, it almost feels like they benefit from this system, where cheaters pay for Premier, get banned, and then simply buy it again.
This clearly serves their financial interests, which is probably why they are not seriously dealing with the issue (this is only my assumption).

Honestly, I don’t think this game is worth playing despite its potential and the graphical improvements made over the years, because the overall experience is terrible.
It’s impossible to truly enjoy the game without encountering at least one or two matches per day filled with bots or hackers.

When playing Premier mode as a new player, you are likely to suffer through more than 10 matches with cheaters just to unlock the ability to see your Premier rating. Even then, hackers with ratings above 10,000 still exist in the game without getting banned.

To sum it up: the game can be fun, but I would not recommend paying anything for it because the overall experience is awful and unfair.

text:
This game feels unfinished and more like a test rather than a fully released game. There are too many cheaters for a game that supposedly has an advanced, AI anticheat (noscope headshots through smokes and walls are legit trust vaclive says so); the first shot as a degree of inaccuracy, leading to some shots that would've hit in GO to not hit in 2; the game is much more noob friendly: pistols rounds are typically 50/50 in 2, you are rarely punished for spamming through smokes, and moving while shooting with a rifle is not as terrible as it was in GO; the game feels slower, and the movement is floaty and weird.
The only two advantages 2 has over GO are its graphics and its smokes. Other than that, GO is better in every way. I can not say how many times I have died in such a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ way, or experienced a large group of cheaters so blatant that Helen Keller could spot them from continents away, that I wish that Global Offensive still had official Valve servers. And I will not lie, all of these issues, which can all be fixed by Valve with a snap of Gabe's godly fingers, make me want to switch to the hell that is Valorant. It is so hard to keep continuing to play such a great game in such a ♥♥♥♥ state, which it really shouldn't be. 
Valve is beating their dying horse-casino hybrid for each last of its final breaths while maggots eat it from the inside-out... and they do not care. They have a perfectly healthy, and though uglier horse, in their possession, they will not change or swap their horses, or at least reform the latter back into an open horse-casino hybrid, despite the latter being so, so much better and so, so much more appealing.

text:
Oh boy, here we go. Counter-Strike 2. The game that was marketed as "the biggest technical leap in CS history" and delivered all the excitement of watching paint dry... if the paint was made by Valve interns who hate you personally. After hyping this thing up for years like it was going to revolutionize shooters, they gave us a reskin that feels like CS:GO after a bad breakup, a lobotomy, and a gambling addiction. 10/10 would rather play Dust2 with a potato than touch this garbage again. Let me count the ways this game has personally offended me.
First off, the Subtick system. Holy mother of god. This was supposed to be the revolutionary feature that made CS feel buttery smooth and fair. Instead it feels like the game is having a stroke every round. You'll peek an angle perfectly, land your shot, and the server decides "nah bro, you actually died 0.3 seconds ago because the universe said so." It's like they took the already questionable netcode of CS:GO, put it in a blender with expired milk, and called it innovation. Sometimes I swear the bullets register based on Valve employee mood swings rather than actual physics.
The guns feel wrong. The AK got nerfed into oblivion, the M4 is somehow even more inconsistent, and don't get me started on the deagle. That beautiful hand cannon that used to make you feel like a god now has the accuracy of a drunk toddler with Parkinson's. I miss the days when economy management actually mattered. Now it's just "buy whatever and hope the random number generator doesn't hate you this round."
The maps. Jesus Christ, the maps. Ancient looks like it was designed by someone who only played on 2006 laptops. Mirage got "updated" but somehow feels worse. And Nuke? My god, they turned Nuke into a vertical nightmare that punishes you for having the audacity to use movement. I genuinely believe some maps were made worse on purpose just to make people buy more cases out of depression.
Speaking of which — the economy and skins. Valve looked at their insanely profitable skin casino and said "how can we make this even more soul-crushing?" The answer was simple: make the game uglier, remove player expressions, and make every animation feel like you're controlling a drunk animatronic. The new character models look like PS3 rejects. My 2015 knife skin now feels like I'm flexing with a participation trophy.
But the absolute worst part? The cheating. Oh my sweet summer child, you thought CS:GO had cheaters? CS2 is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hacker convention with better graphics. VAC is less an anti-cheat and more of a polite suggestion. You'll be in Premier (their "competitive" mode) and some guy named "xX_aimgod420_Xx" with 12 hours played will be 30-0 with perfect flicks while spinning like a washing machine. Valve's solution? "Trust us bro, we're working on it." They've been "working on it" since Obama was president.
The ranking system is a joke too. You win five games in a row? Enjoy your +27. Lose one because your teammate was throwing for the lulz? -89 rating and a swift kick in the nuts. The matchmaking feels like it was designed by a sadist who gets off on making 5-stack friends vs solo queue warriors.
And can we talk about how Valve clearly doesn't play their own game? The communication wheel is garbage, voice chat is filled with either 12-year-olds screaming slurs or dead silence, and the new UI looks like it was designed by someone who only uses mobile games. They removed so many quality-of-life features that CS:GO had for years that it feels like active regression.
The game runs like ♥♥♥♥ on anything but a NASA computer, has input lag that makes you question reality, and somehow still has the same 15-year-old bugs that make you want to throw your monitor out the window. But hey, at least they added... checks notes... new smoke grenades that look slightly different. Revolutionary stuff.
Final verdict: CS2 isn't a sequel. It's a cash grab wearing its predecessor's skin like a horror movie villain. It's the gaming equivalent of your favorite restaurant changing owners, watering down all the recipes, doubling the prices, and then acting surprised when you stop coming.
I'm not saying CS2 is the worst game ever made. I'm just saying I'd rather play Call of Duty with all the microtransactions, pay $70 for a battle pass, and get called the n-word by 40-year-olds than queue another Premier game.
10/10 hate. Masterpiece of disappointment. Valve, if you're reading this (you're not), please just admit you hate your playerbase and put CS:GO back up. Some of us just want to shoot heads in peace without your experimental garbage ruining our mental health.
See you on Dust2 in 2030 when they finally admit defeat.

text:
There is a secret that gives players no peace,
When your accurate shot goes into emptiness.

You aim for the head, the crosshair is like a magnet,
And you see how the enemy trembles from your bullet.
But here he stands, as if nothing happened,
And the blood on the screen is just an illusion.

"Register it, game!" you shout in your heart,
"My shot was accurate, I don't believe in words!"
But the server is silent, and the statistics lie,
And again resentment will grow in your soul.

You see how bullets fly through the enemy,
As if the walls are just your leg.
And it seems as if the world is a dream,
Where the rules of the game are just a strange law.

text:
Honestly, if you’ve ever played CS:GO, jumping into CS2 just feels right. Same classic gameplay, but everything looks and feels sharper. The new smoke mechanics alone are a game changer, grenades actually interact with the environment now, which adds a whole new layer to how you play.

The maps have been beautifully updated, gunplay is as satisfying as ever, and the ranking system finally makes sense. Valve has been consistently patching and improving the game, and it shows.

Yeah, it had a rough launch, but CS2 has grown into exactly what it was supposed to be , the definitive version of the greatest tactical shooter ever made. Free to play, endlessly replayable, and still the most rewarding FPS out there if you’re willing to put in the time.

Just download it. You won’t regret it.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 is a great game… and a great test of your mental stability.
It’s a simple idea: shoot enemies, win rounds, feel good. In practice: you shoot first, die first, and start questioning reality.

One minute you’re chilling, the next you’re in a 1v4 against someone who apparently never misses, even through walls, smoke, and your hopes.

It’s competitive, addictive, and incredibly satisfying when it goes well — which is rare enough to make it special.

Still, nothing really replaces it. You rage, you queue again.
Recommended… but only if your patience has insurance.

text:
“Very good game.

You join the lobby. Spawn CT. The air is cold. Silent.

You turn left.

A Terrorist is floating across the map, gliding through the sky like a biblical angel of death, staring directly into your eyes. Time slows. Your heartbeat fades into the distance.

Suddenly, your life begins replaying before you. Childhood memories. Your family. Friends you haven’t spoken to in years. The love you could’ve had. The future you’ll never see.

The flying T reaches into his pocket and pulls out a Desert Eagle.

One spin.

One shot.

Your teammates collapse instantly, their skulls erupting into pink mist. Then you feel it too — warmth leaving your body as your own thoughts scatter across Mirage like forgotten dreams.

As you fall backwards in slow motion, watching your blood paint the concrete beneath you, you realize this was never a competitive match. It was an execution.

Your vision fades.
Your hearing disappears.
Your final thought echoes through the darkness:

‘anticheat.’”

text: On average, for every 10 players, there are 3 individuals who engage in walling or hacking, regardless of whether they are overt or covert cheaters. This statistic applies to both casual and competitive play. The situation deteriorates further when participating in premier matches, where the player rating exceeds 18k-20k elo, resulting in 4 to 5 hackers for every 10 players. The current condition of this game is disheartening. Please refrain from suggesting that I switch to Faceit; the state of this game is so poor that one must resort to third-party services to achieve a fair gaming experience, which is itself often compromised. While it is understandable to criticize companies like EA, Ubisoft, and Activision/Blizzard, it is equally important to note that fans of Valve and Steam do not hold them accountable with the same intensity. It is quite absurd. Valve and Steam have become a laughingstock. Instead of enhancing their VAC Anti-Cheat system, they focus on releasing cosmetic skins. What are they thinking? If this game had been released without skins and adopted a free-to-play model, it would have been abandoned long ago. The developers at Valve and Steam should be grateful for the current state of the gaming industry, as there is no viable alternative to CS2 aside from the subpar UE5 Slop or Valorant, which I do not consider a direct substitute. Should someone choose to develop their own PC Sandbox game in the style of CS2 using an in-house engine, it would not take much to surpass CS2. The standards within the FPS genre are alarmingly low. CS2 and even Global Offensive are mere shadows of what Source and 1.6 used to represent; they were not flawless, but they were in a far better state than this current iteration. The hit registration, anti-cheat mechanisms, animations, and even the fluidity of the original engine were vastly superior to this dreadful game. Steam and Valve must rectify their issues, as another platform will inevitably surpass them if they do not remove their greedy mindset.

text:
🏆 THE ULTIMATE COMPETITIVE FPS EXPERIENCE
"Counter-Strike 2 is not just a game; it is a discipline where mechanical skill meets psychological warfare."

Having spent thousands of hours across the legacy of Counter-Strike franchise, CS2 feels like both a fresh dawn and a homecoming. Valve has successfully managed to port the core tactical DNA of CS:GO into the Source 2 engine, modernizing the visual and technical foundation without losing the precision the competitive scene demands.

The Good:

Sub-Tick Architecture: While the community had its initial doubts, the sub-tick system offers an incredibly responsive raw input feel once your network settings are properly optimized. What you see is truly what you get.

Dynamic Smokes: The volumetric, reactive smoke grenades completely redefine map control. Being able to open a line of sight with a well-placed HE grenade or by spraying through the smoke adds a massive layer of tactical depth to executes.

Visual & Audio Clarity: Subtraction of visual clutter on revamped maps like Inferno and Mirage significantly improves player visibility. The sound occlusion model provides pinpoint spatial awareness, crucial for clutch situations.

The Grind:
The premier matchmaking rating system keeps the competitive drive alive, though the journey is always a test of mental fortitude. Dealing with sub-optimal trust factors can be exhausting, but climbing the ranks with a dedicated 5-stack is still the most rewarding experience in all of tactical gaming.

If you are looking for a casual shooter to just "relax and unwind", this might test your patience. But if you have the discipline to master recoil patterns, utility lineups, and raw crosshair placement, there is simply no alternative on the market that matches this skill ceiling.

Final Verdict: 9.5 / 10
An absolute masterpiece of competitive integrity.

text:
The game that courageously deleted its own perfect prequel to give us prettier smoke, worse performance, and bullets that disappear into a parallel universe.
It’s the ultimate "tactical" shooter where you don't just fight the enemy—you fight sub-tick rate, desync deaths from behind solid concrete walls, and your own crumbling sanity.
Here is why we are completely trapped by it:
### The Real Tactical Elements
 * **The $1,000 Moving Color Pixels:** Let’s be honest, CS2 isn't a shooter; it’s a highly volatile stock market for weapon skins masquerading as a video game. You will unironically spend $500 on a digital knife that does the exact same damage as the free default one, just so you can frantically flip it back and forth while running to a bomb site.
 * **The Casino Simulator:** Nothing says tactical depth like the overwhelming urge to open a $2.50 weapon case. It's a beautifully disguised gambling addiction where you spend hundreds of dollars chasing a 0.26% chance of a "Gold" drop, only to walk away with twenty identical blue-tier shotguns worth three cents each.
 * **The Premier Spin-Bots:** You’ll sweat your absolute soul out for 11 rounds, only to have a guy named *CaseOpener2026* start spinning like a Beyblade, headshotting your entire team through three walls. The anti-cheat isn't sleeping; it’s just in a permanent coma.
> **The Verdict:** It's a toxic relationship with a digital casino disguised as a competitive shooter. It will ruin your sleep schedule, tank your bank account, and tilt you into oblivion—but you’ll still be back in the matchmaking queue tonight.
> 
Drop me an AWP? I promise I won't lose it immediately.

Needless to say, most of the bad players in this game, take criticism like you're fondling their mother. Steam Support
Rules and Guidelines For Steam: Discussions, Reviews, and User Generated Content
Steam includes a variety of community features that support custom user-generated content. Whether uploading workshop items, personalizing profiles, reviewing products, or posting in the discussions, custom content greatly improves the experience for players in the Steam Community.

Because so many players have access to this content, it is important that it be appropriate for everyone using Steam’s all-ages and international community. We expect that anyone participating in the community make a reasonable judgment about whether their contributions are appropriate.

We also understand that it is challenging to evaluate appropriateness within such a large and diverse community. These rules aim to make that evaluation easier for anyone participating in the community.


Online Conduct

Our Online Conduct rules broadly apply to all behaviors and content on Steam. Content or behaviors that violate these rules are not allowed. Accounts that post such content or engage in such behavior may be subject to restrictions.

The guidelines below are intended to provide more context for how the online conduct rules apply to various areas of Steam, while also including additional specifics for those areas.


Steam Discussions & Comment Threads

There are many shared spaces on Steam where players can engage in public discussion. In addition to the online conduct rules, players and developers should adhere to the following guidelines to ensure that these shared places remain constructive and respectful:

Be respectful of yourself and other players
Interactions with other players should not be abusive, disparaging, or inflammatory, nor create an environment that is unwelcoming.

Examples of disrespectful content include:
Insults or harassment
Threats or encouragement of harm
Trolling or baiting arguments
Discrimination
Bypassing word or content filters
Public accusations towards others

Be constructive and avoid off-topic content
Hubs and discussion threads all have their own purpose & topic. Make sure that contributions are relevant and constructive.

Examples of off-topic content include:
Spamming
Posting topics in incorrect hubs
Re-posting closed, modified or deleted content
Derailing a discussion or topic
Bumping threads

Commercial content is not permitted
Unless otherwise specified, discussions are not a place for commercial activity.

Examples of commercial content include:
Advertisements
Referral promotions
Recruitment
Gambling & raffles
Giveaways with entry requirements

Don't discuss or engage in unlawful or prohibited activity
Any posts or activities that violate Steam's legal agreements are not allowed.

Examples of prohibited conduct include:
Encouraging or facilitating real-world violence
Exploitation of minors
Bypassing Steam's Mature Content Preferences
Doxing or posting personal information of other users
Scamming or deception
Encouraging or engaging in piracy or theft


User Reviews

User reviews give players an opportunity to share their experience with the games they've played, while also enabling other players to make confident and informed purchasing decisions.

Reviewers should adhere to the following guidelines:
Do not direct abuse or insults at other players, developers, or groups.
Do not include threats or encouragement of harm.
Do not use reviews for commercial purposes. Examples include: advertisements, referrals, or promotions.
Do not artificially influence review scores. Examples include: using multiple accounts to leave reviews; coercing other players to leave reviews; or accepting payments or other compensation to leave reviews.
If a copy of the game was received for free, this should be disclosed in the review itself.


User-Uploaded Content

There are many Steam features that support custom image and content uploads for players. Examples include screenshots, player artwork, custom profiles, and workshop items.

When uploading images or workshop content, you should adhere to the following guidelines:
Don’t upload inappropriate content. Examples include:
Real or disturbing depictions of violence;
Content that exploits children in any way; and
Sexually explicit content. This does not forbid game content posted in its own game hub.

Don't bypass Steam's Mature Content Preferences
Don't upload content that you don't have the rights to
Don't upload images of other people without their permission
Don't use content to harass, abuse, or otherwise disparage others
Don’t advertise commercial content
Make sure the content you post matches the space where it is posted. Example: Don’t upload Dota artwork to the Counter-Strike hub.


Steam Community Groups

The Steam Community allows players to create private or public community groups, each with their own discussion hub, announcement system, and group chatroom. The creators and members of these groups are responsible for ensuring that they adhere to all of the guidelines outlined above.

Groups that allow violations of these guidelines may be removed and the accounts involved with uploading the content may be restricted.


Frequently Asked Questions

How are these guidelines enforced?
How can content be reported?
What are Mature Content Preferences?
What if content was removed by mistake?
Why do moderators lock and delete discussions?
What happens when these guidelines are violated?
Why was my content removed when worse stuff exists?

How are these guidelines enforced?
Steam moderators review reported content in conjunction with these guidelines to determine when content should be removed from the community. The examples above give an idea of what type of content is not acceptable, but they are not comprehensive.

How can content be reported?
We encourage players to report any content that they believe to be in violation of these guidelines.

Detailed instructions for how to report each type of content are available in our Reported Content article.

What are Mature Content Preferences?
Every player on Steam has account preferences related to mature content. These preferences determine the types of games and community content that are shown to that player on Steam.

What if my content was removed by mistake?
We understand the challenge of judging the appropriateness of the content that players upload and we’re not always going to get it right. On top of that, subjectivity and context always come into play when applying guidelines to unique content. Feedback from the community helps ensure that we are applying these rules correctly.

If you ever feel that content was mistakenly moderated, you should contact Steam Support. Support can review the content and help you better understand the reasoning or even correct a mistake.

Additionally, moderation messages that accompany the content removal will include a direct link to appeal the decision with Steam Support.

Why do moderators lock and delete discussions?
Respectful arguments are a constructive and beneficial aspect of the Steam Community. When a discussion thread veers off a constructive path or is causing frequent disrespect between players, Steam moderators will lock the thread.

If a discussion thread contains prohibited topics, spam, or is otherwise not constructive, moderators may also delete specific content in an effort to preserve the conversation. However, there are cases where removing the thread itself is necessary.

What happens when these guidelines are violated?
The goal of these rules and guidelines is to create a community that is safe and appropriate for all players. Our priority is to remove content

real or disturbing depictions of violence
content that exploits children in any way and
sexually explicit content. this does not forbid game content posted in its own game hub.

don't bypass steam's mature content preferences
don't upload content that you don't have the rights to
don't upload images of other people without their permission
don't use content to harass abuse or otherwise disparage others
dont advertise commercial content
make sure the content you post matches the space where it is posted. example dont upload dota artwork to the counterstrike hub.


steam community groups

the steam community allows players to create private or public community groups each with their own discussion hub announcement system and group chatroom. the creators and members of these groups are responsible for ensuring that they adhere to all of the guidelines outlined above.

groups that allow violations of these guidelines may be removed and the accounts involved with uploading the content may be restricted.


frequently asked questions

how are these guidelines enforced?
how can content be reported?
what are mature content preferences?
what if content was removed by mistake?
why do moderators lock and delete discussions?
what happens when these guidelines are violated?
why was my content removed when worse stuff exists?

how are these guidelines enforced?
steam moderators review reported content in conjunction with these guidelines to determine when content should be removed from the community. the examples above give an idea of what type of content is not acceptable but they are not comprehensive.

how can content be reported?
we encourage players to report any content that they believe to be in violation of these guidelines.

detailed instructions for how to report each type of content are available in our reported content article.

what are mature content preferences?
every player on steam has account preferences related to mature content. these preferences determine the types of games and community content that are shown to that player on steam.

what if my content was removed by mistake?
we understand the challenge of judging the appropriateness of the content that players upload and were not always going to get it right. on top of that subjectivity and context always come into play when applying guidelines to unique content. feedback from the community helps ensure that we are applying these rules correctly.

if you ever feel that content was mistakenly moderated you should contact steam support. support can review the content and help you better understand the reasoning or even correct a mistake.

additionally moderation messages that accompany the content removal will include a direct link to appeal the decision with steam support.

why do moderators lock and delete discussions?
respectful arguments are a constructive and beneficial aspect of the steam community. when a discussion thread veers off a constructive path or is causing frequent disrespect between players steam moderators will lock the thread.

if a discussion thread contains prohibited topics spam or is otherwise not constructive moderators may also delete specific content in an effort to preserve the conversation. however there are cases where removing the thread itself is necessary.

what happens when these guidelines are violated?
the goal of these rules and guidelines is to create a community that is safe and appropriate for all players. our priority is to remove content

text: CS2 is actually a really good game when you think about it – the gunplay still feels amazing, subtick is finally responsive after you get used to it, the new maps look gorgeous and Premier with the rating system at least gives you some sense of progression. Source 2 was long overdue too and the engine upgrade does alot for the visuals and future modding. BUT, the trust factor system is honestly a nightmare. You never know why you suddenly get lobbies full of smurfs, griefers or blatant cheaters, theres zero transparency, no feedback, nothing. VAC still feels like its two patches behind and im pretty sure half the cheaters i report are still playing weeks later. Valve's communication is basically non-existant aswell, every other update seems to introduce new bugs that stay in the game for weeks before anyone adresses them. If you actually want to play competetive you end up on Faceit anyway, which kinda says everything about the state of official matchmaking. Still thumbs up tho, because when the lobby is decent its still one of the best shooters ever made.

text:
Damn, why haven't I bought this game earlier? This game is actually fun... under conditions:
[list]
[*]You don't play CS2 24/7.
[*]You don't engage with the toxic part of player base.
[*]Actually perform in matches in a way that satisfies you, set your own goals (fx.: achieve that MVP, get a grenade multi-kill etc.)
[*]...while remembering to help your team and play according to the strategy of your teammates. Teamwork is the sole guarantee of victory in CS2, even if your teammates aren't perfect.
[*]BONUS: You get a small monetary bonus for leveling up each week which is nice. Definitely encourages the vast majority of the player base to come back and play the game.
[/list]
Also tip for everyone - If you want to learn some basic grenade throwing and how to initiate it properly, go play Ready or Not. If you want to have better understanding of military tactics in modern shooters, just go and play RoN. You won't regret it. The experience in RoN applies well in CS2.

text:
Dear CS2 Developers,

I truly believe there is something deeply personal going on between me and your servers.

Every time I queue with my friends, I pick up the AWP with confidence, determination, and the false hope that this time my shots will actually count. I aim perfectly, I click, I even see the bullet flying straight through the enemy like a cinematic masterpiece — yet somehow, no damage appears. Nothing. The enemy casually survives as if my AWP is loaded with emotional support rounds instead of bullets.

Meanwhile, on the other side, I die instantly. Not after a fight. Not after a reaction. Instantly. Sometimes I’m already dead before my brain even processes that an enemy existed. It feels less like Counter-Strike and more like a live demonstration of human suffering.

My friends hear me scream “HOW IS THAT NOT A HIT?” at least 14 times per match. At this point, they no longer respond. They simply accept it as part of the game’s ambient audio.

I am not asking to become a pro player overnight. I will still miss easy shots. I will still panic spray. I will still lose rounds I absolutely should win. But I would at least like my bullets — especially the giant expensive AWP bullets — to acknowledge the existence of the enemy player.

Please investigate:

AWP shots visually connecting but doing no damage
Possible desync or hit registration problems
Instant deaths that feel impossible to react to
Whatever ancient curse has specifically targeted my account

I love CS2, but currently my relationship with hit registration feels toxic.

Sincerely,
A tired AWPer trying to believe again
